narrative:
"Last of all comes the tyrannical man; about whom we have once more to ask, how is he formed out of the democratical? And how does he live, in happiness or in misery?"

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "he is the only one remaining."

"There is, however," Socrates said, "a previous question which remains unanswered."

"What question?"

"I do not think that we have adequately determined the nature and number of the appetites, and until this is accomplished the enquiry will always be confused."

"Well," he said, "it is not too late to supply the omission."

"Very true," Socrates said; "and observe the point which I want to understand: Certain of the unnecessary pleasures and appetites I conceive to be unlawful; every one appears to have them, but in some persons they are controlled by the laws and by reason, and the better desires prevail over them -- either they are wholly banished or they become few and weak; while in the case of others they are stronger, and there are more of them."
refashioned:
"Now we come to the last figure in our sequence -- the tyrannical man. How does he develop out of the democratic type? And does he live happily or miserably?"

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "he's the only one we haven't examined yet."

"But there's a prior question we still haven't properly answered."

"What question?"

"We haven't adequately mapped out the nature and variety of human appetites. Until we do, our whole inquiry will remain muddled."

"Well, it's not too late to fix that."

"Good. Here's the key point I want us to understand: There are certain unnecessary pleasures and appetites that I'd call lawless. Everyone has them, but in some people they're kept in check by laws and reason -- the better desires overpower them, and they either vanish entirely or become weak and few. In others, though, these lawless appetites grow stronger and multiply."
elegant: The tyrannical man emerges from the democratic type, but first we must map the lawless appetites. Everyone possesses unlawful desires, yet in some people reason and law suppress them until they become weak or vanish entirely. In others, these same desires grow strong and numerous.
summary: Socrates begins the examination of how the tyrannical man develops from the democratic man, but first insists on clarifying the nature of lawless appetites. Everyone possesses unlawful desires, but they vary in strength depending on whether reason or appetite governs the soul.
distilled: Everyone harbors lawless desires; the difference between people lies in whether reason suppresses or appetite amplifies them.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Transition from Book VIII's democratic man to the tyrannical man -- Continues the degeneration sequence: aristocratic > timocratic > oligarchic > democratic > tyrannical -- The 'unnecessary pleasures' were first distinguished in Book VIII (558d-559d) -- Lawless appetites foreshadow the dream analysis that follows -- Cross-ref: Book IV's tripartite soul (reason, spirit, appetite)

narrative:
"Which appetites do you mean?"

"I mean those which are awake when the reasoning and human and ruling power is asleep; then the wild beast within us, gorged with meat or drink, starts up and having shaken off sleep, goes forth to satisfy his desires; and there is no conceivable folly or crime -- not excepting incest or any other unnatural union, or parricide, or the eating of forbidden food -- which at such a time, when he has parted company with all shame and sense, a man may not be ready to commit."
refashioned:
"Which appetites exactly?"

"The ones that come alive when our reasoning, civilized, governing faculty falls asleep. That's when the wild beast inside us -- bloated with food or drink -- shakes off sleep and goes hunting to satisfy its cravings. At such moments there is no imaginable folly or crime -- not even incest, unnatural acts, parricide, or the eating of forbidden food -- that a person won't be ready to commit, having abandoned all shame and sense."
elegant: The lawless appetites reveal themselves when reason sleeps. Then the wild beast within us, freed from all shame and sense, is ready to commit any conceivable crime -- incest, parricide, or any forbidden act.
summary: Socrates identifies lawless appetites as those that emerge when reason sleeps: the inner 'wild beast,' freed from shame, is capable of any crime including incest, parricide, and forbidden acts.
distilled: When reason sleeps, the wild beast within is capable of every conceivable crime without shame.
notes: Socrates speaking -- The 'wild beast' metaphor is central to Plato's moral psychology -- Anticipates Freud's theory of the unconscious and dream symbolism by over two millennia -- The list of crimes (incest, parricide, forbidden food) recurs in 9.12 as waking acts of the tyrannical man -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 493a-494a on the insatiable soul -- The 'eating of forbidden food' may reference ritual prohibitions or cannibalism taboos

narrative:
"Most true," he said.

"But when a man's pulse is healthy and temperate, and when before going to sleep he has awakened his rational powers, and fed them on noble thoughts and enquiries, collecting himself in meditation; after having first indulged his appetites neither too much nor too little, but just enough to lay them to sleep, and prevent them and their enjoyments and pains from interfering with the higher principle -- which he leaves in the solitude of pure abstraction, free to contemplate and aspire to the knowledge of the unknown, whether in past, present, or future: when again he has allayed the passionate element, if he has a quarrel against any one -- I say, when, after pacifying the two irrational principles, he rouses up the third, which is reason, before he takes his rest, then, as you know, he attains truth most nearly, and is least likely to be the sport of fantastic and lawless visions."
refashioned:
"Absolutely true."

"But consider the opposite case. When a man is healthy and moderate, and before sleep he has engaged his rational mind with noble thoughts and inquiry, gathering himself in reflection; when he has satisfied his appetites just enough to quiet them -- neither starving them nor overfeeding them -- so they won't disturb the higher faculty; when he leaves that higher faculty free in pure contemplation to reach toward knowledge of what it doesn't yet understand, whether past, present, or future; and when he has also calmed his spirited element, settling any grudges -- when, having quieted these two irrational parts, he activates the third, reason, before resting: then he comes closest to truth, and his dreams are least likely to be lawless and fantastical."
elegant: The person who quiets both appetite and spirit before sleep, engaging reason through noble contemplation, comes closest to truth in their dreams. Balanced moderation of the lower parts frees the rational faculty for genuine insight.
summary: Socrates describes the ideal preparation for sleep: moderate the appetites, calm the spirited element, and activate reason through contemplation. Under these conditions, a person's dreams approach truth rather than lawless fantasy.
distilled: When reason is nourished and appetite quieted before sleep, dreams become vehicles for truth rather than lawless fantasy.
notes: Socrates speaking -- This passage is Plato's theory of dream psychology and rational dreaming -- The three parts of the soul from Book IV recur: appetite, spirit (passion), reason -- 'Pure abstraction' (auto kath' hauto) connects to the Forms discussed in Books V-VII -- The technique described resembles contemplative practices found in Pythagorean traditions -- Cross-ref: Philebus on mixed vs. pure pleasures -- Anticipates the divided line and philosopher's ascent of Book VII

narrative:
"In saying this I have been running into a digression; but the point which I desire to note is that in all of us, even in good men, there is a lawless wild-beast nature, which peers out in sleep. Pray, consider whether I am right, and you agree with me."

"Yes, I agree."

"And now remember the character which we attributed to the democratic man. He was supposed from his youth upwards to have been trained under a miserly parent, who encouraged the saving appetites in him, but discountenanced the unnecessary, which aim only at amusement and ornament?"
refashioned:
"I've been digressing. But the essential point is this: in all of us, even the best of us, there lurks a lawless, beastly nature that reveals itself in sleep. Do you agree?"

"Yes, I agree."

"Good. Now recall the character we gave the democratic man. We said he was raised from youth under a miserly father who encouraged the money-saving appetites but suppressed the unnecessary ones -- those aimed only at amusement and luxury."
elegant: In all of us, even the virtuous, a lawless beast-nature lurks and reveals itself in sleep. Returning to the main argument: the democratic man was raised by an oligarchic father who cultivated thrift and suppressed unnecessary pleasures.
summary: Socrates concludes his digression on dreams by affirming that even good people harbor a lawless inner nature visible in sleep. He then returns to the democratic man's origin: raised by a miserly oligarchic father who suppressed unnecessary pleasures.
distilled: Even good people harbor a lawless beast-nature revealed in sleep; the democratic man was raised under an oligarchic father's thrift.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Even in good men' universalizes the lawless appetites -- a key psychological insight -- Transitions back from the dream digression to the main genealogy of character types -- The democratic man's origin under an oligarchic father was established in Book VIII (558c-562a) -- 'Saving appetites' vs. 'unnecessary appetites' maps onto the oligarchic soul's internal conflict -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on the oligarchic man's character

narrative:
"True. And then he got into the company of a more refined, licentious sort of people, and taking to all their wanton ways rushed into the opposite extreme from an abhorrence of his father's meanness. At last, being a better man than his corruptors, he was drawn in both directions until he halted midway and led a life, not of vulgar and slavish passion, but of what he deemed moderate indulgence in various pleasures. After this manner the democrat was generated out of the oligarch?"

"Yes," he said; "that was our view of him, and is so still."

"And now," Socrates said, "years will have passed away, and you must conceive this man, such as he is, to have a son, who is brought up in his father's principles."
refashioned:
"Right. Then this young man fell in with a more sophisticated, pleasure-seeking crowd. Revolted by his father's stinginess, he swung to the opposite extreme, embracing their indulgent ways. But since he was fundamentally better than his corruptors, he was pulled in both directions until he settled in the middle -- living not a life of crude, slavish passion, but what he considered moderate enjoyment of varied pleasures. That's how the democratic man emerged from the oligarchic one?"

"Yes, that was our account, and it still holds."

"Now, imagine years have passed. This democratic man has a son, raised according to his father's principles."
elegant: The democratic man, reacting against his oligarchic father's miserliness, settled into what he considered moderate indulgence. Now imagine this man has a son, raised on those same democratic principles.
summary: The democratic man emerged as a compromise between his oligarchic father's thrift and the licentiousness of his companions, settling into moderate indulgence. Now Socrates asks us to imagine this man's son, raised on democratic principles.
distilled: The democratic man's son, raised on principles of moderate indulgence, becomes the raw material for tyrannical transformation.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Recap of Book VIII's account of the democratic man's formation (559d-561e) -- 'Better man than his corruptors' -- the democratic man retains some moral sense -- The generational pattern continues: each regime-type produces a son who degenerates further -- 'Moderate indulgence in various pleasures' characterizes democratic equality of desires -- The son of the democrat will become the tyrant -- the final stage of degeneration

narrative:
"I can imagine him."

"Then you must further imagine the same thing to happen to the son which has already happened to the father: he is drawn into a perfectly lawless life, which by his seducers is termed 'perfect liberty'; and his father and friends take part with his moderate desires, and the opposite party assist the opposite ones. As soon as these dire magicians and tyrant-makers find that they are losing their hold on him, they contrive to implant in him a master passion, to be lord over his idle and spendthrift lusts -- a sort of monstrous winged drone -- that is the only image which will adequately describe him."
refashioned:
"I can picture him."

"Then imagine the same pattern repeating with the son. He's drawn into a completely lawless lifestyle, which his seducers call 'perfect freedom.' His father and moderate friends pull him one way; the opposite faction pulls him the other. When these sinister manipulators -- these tyrant-makers -- sense they're losing their grip on him, they engineer a master passion to implant in his soul, one that will rule over all his idle, wasteful desires. Picture it as a kind of monstrous winged drone -- that's the only image that fits."
elegant: The democrat's son is drawn into lawlessness that his seducers call 'perfect freedom.' When the tyrant-makers feel their hold slipping, they implant a master passion in him -- a monstrous winged drone that lords over all other desires.
summary: The democratic man's son is seduced into lawlessness disguised as freedom. When his corruptors fear losing influence, they implant a dominant master passion -- likened to a monstrous winged drone -- to rule over all his other desires.
distilled: Tyrant-makers implant a master passion -- a monstrous drone -- to dominate all other desires in the proto-tyrannical soul.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Perfect liberty' as a euphemism for lawlessness -- central to Plato's critique of radical democracy -- 'Dire magicians and tyrant-makers' refers to demagogues and flatterers -- The 'monstrous winged drone' develops the drone metaphor from Book VIII (552c, 564b) -- The 'master passion' (eros) is the tyrant within the soul -- Cross-ref: Book VIII's drones (stingless and stinging) as parasitic elements -- The drone image connects appetite to parasitism

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the only adequate image of him."

"And when his other lusts, amid clouds of incense and perfumes and garlands and wines, and all the pleasures of a dissolute life, now let loose, come buzzing around him, nourishing to the utmost the sting of desire which they implant in his drone-like nature, then at last this lord of the soul, having Madness for the captain of his guard, breaks out into a frenzy: and if he finds in himself any good opinions or appetites in process of formation, and there is in him any sense of shame remaining, to these better principles he puts an end, and casts them forth until he has purged away temperance and brought in madness to the full."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's the only image that does him justice."

"And when his other lusts -- set loose amid clouds of incense, perfumes, garlands, wine, and every dissolute pleasure -- come buzzing around him, feeding the sting of desire in his drone-like nature to its maximum intensity, then this master passion, with Madness as captain of its bodyguard, erupts into frenzy. If it discovers any good opinions or healthy appetites still forming within him, any lingering trace of shame, it destroys them. It drives out every better impulse until it has purged the soul of all temperance and filled it with sheer madness."
elegant: The master passion, surrounded by dissolute pleasures, grows to frenzy with Madness as its bodyguard captain. It purges the soul of every remaining good impulse, driving out temperance and replacing it with complete madness.
summary: The tyrannical soul's master passion, nourished by dissolute pleasures, erupts into frenzy with Madness as its captain. It systematically destroys all remaining good opinions and shame, purging temperance and replacing it with total madness.
distilled: The master passion, captained by Madness, purges every trace of temperance from the soul and replaces it with frenzy.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Personification of Madness (Mania) as 'captain of the guard' parallels political bodyguards of actual tyrants -- The buzzing lusts extend the drone/bee metaphor -- 'Purged away temperance and brought in madness' inverts the philosophical purgation of the soul -- Clouds of incense, perfumes, garlands evoke Dionysian revelry and symposium culture -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 244a-245c on divine vs. pathological madness -- The tyrannical soul undergoes a dark parody of philosophical conversion

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the way in which the tyrannical man is generated."

"And is not this the reason why of old love has been called a tyrant?"

"I should not wonder."

"Further," Socrates said, "has not a drunken man also the spirit of a tyrant?"

"He has."

"And you know that a man who is deranged and not right in his mind, will fancy that he is able to rule, not only over men, but also over the gods?"

"That he will."

"And the tyrannical man in the true sense of the word comes into being when, either under the influence of nature, or habit, or both, he becomes drunken, lustful, passionate? O my friend, is not that so?"

"Assuredly."

"Such is the man and such is his origin. And next, how does he live?"

"Suppose, as people facetiously say, you were to tell me."

"I imagine," Socrates said, "at the next step in his progress, that there will be feasts and carousals and revellings and courtezans, and all that sort of thing; Love is the lord of the house within him, and orders all the concerns of his soul."

"That is certain."

"Yes; and every day and every night desires grow up many and formidable, and their demands are many."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly how the tyrannical man comes into being."

"And isn't this why love has traditionally been called a tyrant?"

"That wouldn't surprise me."

"And doesn't a drunk man also have the spirit of a tyrant?"

"He does."

"And you know that a deranged person imagines he can rule not only over other people but even over the gods?"

"Absolutely."

"So the truly tyrannical man comes into being when, through nature or habit or both, he becomes drunk with lust and passion. Isn't that right, my friend?"

"It is."

"That's his character and his origin. Now -- how does he actually live?"

"Why don't you tell me, as the saying goes."

"I imagine the next stage involves feasts, drinking parties, orgies, and prostitutes -- the whole catalogue of debauchery. Erotic love is master of the house within him, directing everything in his soul."

"No doubt."

"And every day and every night, new desires multiply, growing fiercer and more demanding."
elegant: Love has always been called a tyrant. The tyrannical man, made drunk by lust through nature or habit, lives a life of feasts and debauchery. Eros rules his soul as master of the house, and his desires multiply daily, growing ever more demanding.
summary: Socrates draws parallels between the tyrannical soul and the states of erotic obsession, drunkenness, and madness. The tyrannical man, driven by Eros as master of his soul, lives amid constant feasting and debauchery, with desires multiplying daily.
distilled: Eros rules the tyrannical soul like a despot, filling life with debauchery as desires multiply without limit.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Love has been called a tyrant' references Greek poetic tradition (Sophocles, Euripides) -- Eros as tyrant of the soul is Plato's master metaphor for the tyrannical personality -- The triad of drunkenness, lust, and madness maps onto appetite, eros, and irrationality -- 'Rule over the gods' indicates hubris (cf. Xerxes in Herodotus) -- Cross-ref: Symposium 177d on Eros as powerful god; Phaedrus on the two horses of the soul -- The facetious tone ('suppose you were to tell me') shows the dialogue's informal register

narrative:
"They are indeed," he said.

"His revenues, if he has any, are soon spent."

"True."

"Then comes debt and the cutting down of his property."

"Of course."

"When he has nothing left, must not his desires, crowding in the nest like young ravens, be crying aloud for food; and he, goaded on by them, and especially by love himself, who is in a manner the captain of them, is in a frenzy, and would fain discover whom he can defraud or despoil of his property, in order that he may gratify them?"
refashioned:
"They certainly are."

"Whatever income he has is quickly spent."

"True."

"Then come debts and the liquidation of his property."

"Naturally."

"And when he has nothing left, won't his desires -- crowding in the nest like hungry young ravens crying out for food -- goad him on? With Eros himself as their ringleader, he falls into a frenzy, desperately searching for someone to defraud or rob in order to feed those cravings."
elegant: The tyrannical man burns through his money, falls into debt, and liquidates his property. His desires, like hungry ravens in a nest, drive him to frenzy -- with Eros as their captain -- seeking anyone to defraud or rob.
summary: The tyrannical man quickly exhausts his resources, falls into debt, and sells off property. His insatiable desires, led by Eros, then drive him to seek victims to defraud and rob.
distilled: Bankrupted by insatiable desire, the tyrannical man turns to fraud and theft under the captaincy of Eros.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- The raven simile (young ravens crying for food) emphasizes the insatiable, predatory nature of desire -- Economic ruin as the first consequence of tyrannical character -- 'Love himself, who is in a manner the captain' continues the Eros-as-tyrant metaphor -- The progression: spend > debt > sell property > defraud others mirrors political tyranny's economic trajectory -- Cross-ref: Book VIII on the tyrant's need to keep making war to maintain power

narrative:
"Yes, that is sure to be the case."

"He must have money, no matter how, if he is to escape horrid pains and pangs."

"He must."

"And as in himself there was a succession of pleasures, and the new got the better of the old and took away their rights, so he being younger will claim to have more than his father and his mother, and if he has spent his own share of the property, he will take a slice of theirs."

"No doubt he will."

"And if his parents will not give way, then he will try first of all to cheat and deceive them."

"Very true."

"And if he fails, then he will use force and plunder them."

"Yes, probably."

"And if the old man and woman fight for their own, what then, my friend? Will the creature feel any compunction at tyrannizing over them?"

"Nay," he said, "I should not feel at all comfortable about his parents."
refashioned:
"That's certain to happen."

"He must have money by any means necessary, or face terrible suffering."

"He must."

"And just as within himself new pleasures displaced old ones and seized their place, so this younger man will claim a larger share than his parents. Once he's blown through his own inheritance, he'll help himself to theirs."

"No doubt."

"And if they won't hand it over, he'll first try to cheat and manipulate them."

"Absolutely."

"And if that fails, he'll use force and simply take it."

"Probably, yes."

"And if the old man and woman fight back to protect what's theirs -- will this creature feel any guilt about tyrannizing his own parents?"

"I wouldn't feel at all safe if I were his parents."
elegant: Desperate for money, the tyrannical man first exhausts his own inheritance, then cheats his parents, then robs them by force. Just as new pleasures displaced old ones within his soul, the son displaces his parents' rights without compunction.
summary: The tyrannical man, desperate for funds, progresses from spending his own inheritance to cheating his parents, to robbing them by force. He feels no compunction about tyrannizing those who raised him.
distilled: The tyrannical man's escalating desperation leads him from fraud to violence against his own parents without remorse.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- The internal logic of appetite mirrors external behavior: new pleasures displace old ones, so the son displaces parents' rights -- The escalation (cheat > deceive > force > plunder) shows moral degeneration as a progressive sequence -- 'The creature' (to therion) dehumanizes the tyrannical man -- Adeimantus' discomfort ('not feel at all comfortable') shows the emotional weight of the argument -- Cross-ref: Book IV on justice as each part doing its own work vs. this inversion -- Parricide theme connects to 9.2's dream-crimes becoming waking reality

narrative:
"But, O heavens! Adeimantus, on account of some newfangled love of a harlot, who is anything but a necessary connection, can you believe that he would strike the mother who is his ancient friend and necessary to his very existence, and would place her under the authority of the other, when she is brought under the same roof with her; or that, under like circumstances, he would do the same to his withered old father, first and most indispensable of friends, for the sake of some newly found blooming youth who is the reverse of indispensable?"

"Yes, indeed," he said; "I believe that he would."

"Truly, then," Socrates said, "a tyrannical son is a blessing to his father and mother."
refashioned:
"But good heavens, Adeimantus! Can you believe that for the sake of some new infatuation with a prostitute -- someone entirely unnecessary to his life -- he would actually strike his own mother, his oldest friend, essential to his very existence? That he'd subject her to the authority of this newcomer under the same roof? Or that he'd treat his frail old father the same way -- his first and most indispensable companion -- all for some fresh young lover who is anything but indispensable?"

"Yes, I genuinely believe he would."

"What a blessing, then, is a tyrannical son to his father and mother!"
elegant: The tyrannical man would strike his own mother and subjugate his aged father -- his oldest, most essential bonds -- for the sake of a passing infatuation with someone entirely dispensable. A tyrannical son is quite the blessing to his parents.
summary: Socrates expresses horror that the tyrannical man would abuse his own parents for the sake of a new lover or prostitute, subordinating his deepest family bonds to fleeting desire. He calls a tyrannical son a 'blessing' with bitter irony.
distilled: The tyrannical man would abuse his own parents to serve a passing infatuation, subordinating essential bonds to dispensable desires.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'O heavens!' marks Socrates' rhetorical outrage at the moral enormity -- The contrast between 'ancient friend and necessary' (mother) vs. 'newfangled' and 'reverse of indispensable' (lover) sharpens the irony -- 'A tyrannical son is a blessing' is bitter Socratic irony -- The abuse of parents fulfills the parricide threatened in 9.2's dream-crimes -- Cross-ref: Laws 881a-882c on penalties for striking parents -- The passage dramatizes how eros-tyranny inverts natural bonds of philia

narrative:
"He is indeed," he replied.

"He first takes their property, and when that falls, and pleasures are beginning to swarm in the hive of his soul, then he breaks into a house, or steals the garments of some nightly wayfarer; next he proceeds to clear a temple. Meanwhile the old opinions which he had when a child, and which gave judgment about good and evil, are overthrown by those others which have just been emancipated, and are now the bodyguard of love and share his empire. These in his democratic days, when he was still subject to the laws and to his father, were only let loose in the dreams of sleep. But now that he is under the dominion of love, he becomes always and in waking reality what he was then very rarely and in a dream only; he will commit the foulest murder, or eat forbidden food, or be guilty of any other horrid act. Love is his tyrant, and lives lordly in him and lawlessly, and being himself a king, leads him on, as a tyrant leads a State, to the performance of any reckless deed by which he can maintain himself and the rabble of his associates, whether those whom evil communications have brought in from without, or those whom he himself has allowed to break loose within him by reason of a similar evil nature in himself. Have we not here a picture of his way of life?"
refashioned:
"He certainly is."

"First he takes his parents' property. When that runs out and pleasures keep swarming in the hive of his soul, he breaks into houses, robs travelers at night, and plunders temples. Meanwhile, the moral convictions he held as a child -- his sense of good and evil -- are overthrown by the newly liberated desires that now serve as the bodyguard of Eros and share his tyranny. In his democratic days, when he still obeyed laws and respected his father, these desires only emerged in dreams. But now, under the dominion of Eros, he becomes in waking life what he previously was only rarely, in sleep: he commits the foulest murders, eats forbidden food, and is capable of any atrocity. Love is his tyrant, ruling within him like a king, driving him to every reckless act needed to sustain itself and the mob of desires -- whether imported from corrupt company or unleashed from within by his own corrupt nature. Isn't this the picture of how he lives?"
elegant: After exhausting his parents' wealth, the tyrannical man turns to burglary, robbery, and temple-plundering. His childhood moral convictions are overthrown by desires that now guard Eros. What once surfaced only in dreams becomes waking reality: murder, sacrilege, every atrocity. Love rules him as tyrant, driving every reckless act.
summary: The tyrannical man, having depleted family resources, turns to crime -- housebreaking, robbery, temple-plundering. His childhood morality is overthrown by desires serving Eros. The crimes that once appeared only in dreams become his waking reality, as Love rules him like an internal tyrant.
distilled: The tyrannical man's dream-crimes become waking reality as Eros overthrows childhood morality and rules the soul as absolute tyrant.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Central passage connecting the dream analysis (9.2-9.3) to waking tyrannical behavior -- 'Hive of his soul' continues the bee/drone imagery from 9.6-9.7 -- 'Bodyguard of love' parallels the tyrant's political bodyguard (Book VIII, 566b-567d) -- Temple robbery was among the gravest crimes in Athens -- 'What he was in a dream only' is the key thesis: the tyrannical man lives the nightmare -- Cross-ref: Book II's challenge (360b-362c) on the unjust man who appears just -- 'Evil communications' echoes Menander's famous line later quoted by Paul (1 Corinthians 15:33)

narrative:
"Yes, indeed," he said.

"And if there are only a few of them in the State, the rest of the people are well disposed, they go away and become the bodyguard or mercenary soldiers of some other tyrant who may probably want them for a war; and if there is no war, they stay at home and do many little pieces of mischief in the city."

"What sort of mischief?"

"For example, they are the thieves, burglars, cutpurses, footpads, robbers of temples, man-stealers of the community; or if they are able to speak they turn informers, and bear false witness, and take bribes."

"A small catalogue of evils, even if the perpetrators of them are few in number."
refashioned:
"Yes, indeed."

"Now, if there are only a few of these tyrannical types in a state and the rest of the population is well-disposed, they leave and become bodyguards or mercenaries for some other tyrant who needs soldiers for a war. If there's no war to fight, they stay home and commit all manner of petty crimes in the city."

"What kind of crimes?"

"They become the city's thieves, burglars, pickpockets, muggers, temple-robbers, and kidnappers. If they're articulate, they become informers who bear false witness and take bribes."

"That's quite a catalogue of evils, even if the criminals are few."
elegant: When few tyrannical men exist in a state, they become mercenaries abroad or criminals at home -- thieves, burglars, pickpockets, muggers, temple-robbers, kidnappers, false witnesses, and bribe-takers.
summary: When only a few tyrannical types exist in a city, they either serve as mercenaries for foreign tyrants or remain as domestic criminals -- thieves, robbers, kidnappers, false witnesses, and bribe-takers.
distilled: Tyrannical individuals who lack political power become mercenaries abroad or criminals at home.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- Transition from the individual tyrannical man to his social role -- 'Bodyguard or mercenary soldiers' connects to the tyrant's need for armed supporters (Book VIII, 567d-568a) -- 'Man-stealers' (andrapodistai) refers to slave-traders/kidnappers -- 'If they are able to speak' distinguishes between violent criminals and intellectual criminals (corrupt orators, sycophants) -- The sycophant (professional informer/false accuser) was a well-known Athenian social type -- 'A small catalogue of evils' is Adeimantus' ironic understatement

narrative: "Yes," Socrates said; "but small and great are comparative terms, and all these things, in the misery and evil which they inflict upon a State, do not come within a thousand miles of the tyrant; when this noxious class and their followers grow numerous and become conscious of their strength, assisted by the infatuation of the people, they choose from among themselves the one who has most of the tyrant in his own soul, and him they create their tyrant."
refashioned: "Yes, but 'small' and 'great' are relative terms. All these crimes, in the misery and evil they inflict on a state, don't come within a thousand miles of what happens when a full tyrant emerges. When this toxic class and their followers grow numerous enough to become aware of their collective strength -- aided by the people's own delusion -- they select from among themselves the one with the most tyrannical soul, and they make him their tyrant."
elegant: These individual crimes pale beside full-blown tyranny. When the tyrannical class grows numerous and conscious of its strength, aided by the people's delusion, they choose the most tyrannical soul among them as their ruler.
summary: Individual tyrannical criminals are minor compared to full political tyranny. When enough such people gather and recognize their collective power, aided by popular delusion, they elevate the most tyrannical soul among them to be their tyrant.
distilled: When tyrannical individuals reach critical mass, aided by popular delusion, they elevate the most tyrannical soul to absolute power.
notes: Socrates speaking -- Bridges the individual tyrannical character to political tyranny -- 'Infatuation of the people' (aphrosyne tou demou) -- the masses' complicity in tyranny's rise -- 'Most of the tyrant in his own soul' -- the worst soul gets the most power, an anti-meritocracy -- Cross-ref: Book VIII (565c-566d) on how the people's champion becomes tyrant -- Echoes Thucydides on the rise of demagogues during democratic crises -- The selection mechanism inverts the philosopher-king ideal of Books V-VII

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "and he will be the most fit to be a tyrant."

"If the people yield, well and good; but if they resist him, as he began by beating his own father and mother, so now, if he has the power, he beats them, and will keep his dear old fatherland or motherland, as the Cretans say, in subjection to his young retainers whom he has introduced to be their rulers and masters. This is the end of his passions and desires."

"Exactly."

"When such men are only private individuals and before they get power, this is their character; they associate entirely with their own flatterers or ready tools; or if they want anything from anybody, they in their turn are equally ready to bow down before them: they profess every sort of affection for them; but when they have gained their point they know them no more."

"Yes, truly."

"They are always either the masters or servants and never the friends of anybody; the tyrant never tastes of true freedom or friendship."

"Certainly not."

"And may we not rightly call such men treacherous?"

"No question."

"Also they are utterly unjust, if we were right in our notion of justice?"

"Yes," he said, "and we were perfectly right."

"Let us then sum up in a word," Socrates said, "the character of the worst man: he is the waking reality of what we dreamed."

"Most true."

"And this is he who being by nature most of a tyrant bears rule, and the longer he lives the more of a tyrant he becomes."
refashioned:
"Yes, he'll be the most naturally suited to be a tyrant."

"If the people submit, fine. But if they resist -- well, just as he started by beating his own father and mother, he'll now beat his fatherland if he has the power. He'll keep his 'dear old motherland,' as the Cretans put it, in subjection to the young enforcers he's imported as its rulers and masters. This is where his passions and desires ultimately lead."

"Exactly."

"Before such men gain power, while they're still private citizens, here's what they're like: they surround themselves exclusively with flatterers and lackeys. If they need something from someone, they're perfectly willing to grovel before them, professing every kind of affection. But the moment they've gotten what they want, they don't know these people anymore."

"That's exactly right."

"They are always either masters or servants, never the friends of anyone. The tyrant never experiences true freedom or genuine friendship."

"Never."

"Can we rightly call such men treacherous?"

"Without question."

"And utterly unjust, if our definition of justice was correct?"

"Yes -- and our definition was perfectly correct."

"Then let me sum up the character of the worst man in a single phrase: he is the waking reality of what we only dreamed."

"Exactly right."

"And this is the man who, being the most tyrannical by nature, holds power -- and the longer he rules, the more tyrannical he becomes."
elegant: The tyrant treats his fatherland as he treated his parents -- with violence. He knows only masters and servants, never friends, never true freedom. He is treacherous, utterly unjust, and the waking reality of what we only dreamed. The longer he rules, the worse he becomes.
summary: The tyrannical man, if he gains power, subjugates his country as he did his parents. He is incapable of friendship, knowing only domination or servility. Socrates sums up: the worst man is the waking embodiment of humanity's darkest dreams, and power only makes him worse over time.
distilled: The worst man is the waking reality of our darkest dreams: incapable of friendship, knowing only domination, and worsening with every day in power.
notes: Socrates and Adeimantus speaking -- 'Waking reality of what we dreamed' ties together the dream analysis (9.2-9.4) with the tyrannical man's portrait -- 'Dear old fatherland/motherland' (Cretan expression) parallels the literal father and mother he abused (9.10-9.11) -- 'Never the friends of anybody' anticipates Aristotle's similar analysis in Politics V -- 'Either masters or servants' defines tyranny as the negation of political friendship (philia) -- 'The longer he lives the more of a tyrant he becomes' -- power corrupts progressively -- Cross-ref: Book II's challenge about the unjust man who appears just (360b-362c) -- Marks the end of the character portrait and transition to the happiness question

narrative:
"That is certain," said Glaucon, taking his turn to answer.

"And will not he who has been shown to be the wickedest, be also the most miserable? And he who has tyrannized longest and most, most continually and truly miserable; although this may not be the opinion of men in general?"

"Yes," he said, "inevitably."

"And must not the tyrannical man be like the tyrannical State, and the democratical man like the democratical State; and the same of the others?"

"Certainly."

"And as State is to State in virtue and happiness, so is man in relation to man?"

"To be sure."

"Then comparing our original city, which was under a king, and the city which is under a tyrant, how do they stand as to virtue?"

"They are the opposite extremes," he said, "for one is the very best and the other is the very worst."
refashioned:
"That is certain," said Glaucon, taking his turn to answer.

"And won't the man shown to be the most wicked also be the most miserable? And the one who has been a tyrant longest and most completely will be the most continuously and genuinely miserable -- even if that's not what most people believe?"

"Yes, inevitably."

"And the tyrannical man must resemble the tyrannical state, just as the democratic man resembles the democratic state, and so on for the others?"

"Of course."

"And as one state compares to another in virtue and happiness, so does one type of person compare to another?"

"Certainly."

"Then compare our original city -- the one ruled by a philosopher-king -- with the city under a tyrant. Where do they stand in terms of virtue?"

"They are polar opposites: one is the very best, the other the very worst."
elegant: The wickedest man is also the most miserable, and the longest-reigning tyrant the most continuously so. As state parallels individual, the kingly city and the tyrannical city are polar opposites in virtue -- one the best, the other the worst.
summary: Glaucon takes over as interlocutor. Socrates establishes that the wickedest man is the most miserable, that individuals mirror their corresponding state-types, and that the kingly and tyrannical cities represent the extremes of virtue and vice.
distilled: The most wicked man is the most miserable; the philosopher-king's city and the tyrant's city are the extremes of virtue and vice.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon now speaking (Glaucon replaces Adeimantus) -- Begins the FIRST PROOF that justice is superior to injustice: the argument from the city-soul analogy -- The five regime types ranked: royal/aristocratic, timocratic, oligarchic, democratic, tyrannical -- 'Although this may not be the opinion of men in general' acknowledges popular opinion favors the tyrant -- Cross-ref: Book II (358a-362c) where Glaucon and Adeimantus challenged Socrates to prove justice superior -- The city-soul analogy was established in Book II (368c-369a) -- This begins Socrates' systematic response to Book II's challenge

narrative:
"There can be no mistake," Socrates said, "as to which is which, and therefore I will at once enquire whether you would arrive at a similar decision about their relative happiness and misery. And here we must not allow ourselves to be panic-stricken at the apparition of the tyrant, who is only a unit and may perhaps have a few retainers about him; but let us go as we ought into every corner of the city and look all about, and then we will give our opinion."

"A fair invitation," he replied; "and I see, as every one must, that a tyranny is the wretchedest form of government, and the rule of a king the happiest."
refashioned:
"There can be no confusion about which is which. So let me now ask: would you reach a similar verdict about their relative happiness and misery? And here we must not be intimidated by the outward spectacle of the tyrant, who is just one person with perhaps a few retainers around him. We need to go into every corner of the city and examine everything before reaching our verdict."

"That's a fair approach. And I can see, as everyone must, that tyranny is the most wretched form of government, and kingship the happiest."
elegant: Do not be dazzled by the tyrant's outward spectacle -- examine every corner of the city. Tyranny is the wretchedest form of government, kingship the happiest.
summary: Socrates warns against being dazzled by the tyrant's external appearance and insists on examining the city thoroughly. Glaucon agrees that tyranny is the most wretched form of government and kingship the happiest.
distilled: Look past the tyrant's spectacle to the city's reality: tyranny is the most wretched government, kingship the happiest.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Panic-stricken at the apparition' warns against being deceived by the tyrant's outward grandeur -- a key epistemological theme -- 'Only a unit' (heis monos) emphasizes the tyrant's isolation -- 'Every corner of the city' insists on comprehensive inquiry, not superficial impressions -- Cross-ref: Book VII's Cave allegory -- those who return must look past appearances -- Glaucon's ready agreement prepares for the deeper analysis to come -- The method of thorough inspection connects to the philosopher's duty to examine reality

narrative: "And in estimating the men too, may I not fairly make a like request, that I should have a judge whose mind can enter into and see through human nature? He must not be like a child who looks at the outside and is dazzled at the pompous aspect which the tyrannical nature assumes to the beholder, but let him be one who has a clear insight. May I suppose that the judgment is given in the hearing of us all by one who is able to judge, and has dwelt in the same place with him, and been present at his daily life and known him in his family relations, where he may be seen stripped of his tragedy attire, and again in the hour of public danger -- he shall tell us about the happiness and misery of the tyrant when compared with other men?"
refashioned: "And in judging the men themselves, may I make the same request? I want a judge whose mind can penetrate human nature. Not someone who, like a child, looks only at exteriors and is dazzled by the pompous facade the tyrannical man presents to the world. Let the judge be someone with genuine insight -- someone who has lived alongside the tyrant, witnessed his daily life, known him in his private family relations where he is stripped of his theatrical costume, and also seen him in moments of public crisis. Let this person tell us about the tyrant's happiness and misery compared with other men."
elegant: Judge the tyrant not by his public spectacle but through the eyes of someone who has seen him in private -- in family life, stripped of theatrical costume, and in moments of crisis. Only such a judge can assess his true happiness.
summary: Socrates demands a judge of the tyrant who can see past outward appearances -- one who has witnessed the tyrant in private life, family relations, and public danger, stripped of his theatrical facade.
distilled: Only a judge who has seen the tyrant stripped of his public facade in private moments can assess his true happiness.
notes: Socrates speaking -- 'Tragedy attire' (tragike skene) explicitly compares the tyrant to a theatrical character -- appearance vs. reality -- 'Like a child' who is dazzled by appearances recalls the Cave's prisoners (Book VII) -- The demand for an experienced, penetrating judge anticipates the philosopher-judge theme of the Second Proof -- 'Family relations' where he is 'stripped' connects to 9.10-9.11's abuse of parents -- Cross-ref: Book II (360e-361d) on the perfectly unjust man who maintains the appearance of justice -- The theatrical metaphor runs throughout the Republic

narrative:
"That again," he said, "is a very fair proposal."

"Shall I assume that we ourselves are able and experienced judges and have before now met with such a person? We shall then have some one who will answer our enquiries."

"By all means."

"Let me ask you not to forget the parallel of the individual and the State; bearing this in mind, and glancing in turn from one to the other of them, will you tell me their respective conditions?"

"What do you mean?" he asked.

"Beginning with the State," Socrates replied, "would you say that a city which is governed by a tyrant is free or enslaved?"

"No city," he said, "can be more completely enslaved."

"And yet, as you see, there are freemen as well as masters in such a State?"

"Yes," he said, "I see that there are -- a few; but the people, speaking generally, and the best of them, are miserably degraded and enslaved."

"Then if the man is like the State," Socrates said, "must not the same rule prevail? His soul is full of meanness and vulgarity -- the best elements in him are enslaved; and there is a small ruling part, which is also the worst and maddest."

"Inevitably."

"And would you say that the soul of such an one is the soul of a freeman, or of a slave?"

"He has the soul of a slave, in my opinion."

"And the State which is enslaved under a tyrant is utterly incapable of acting voluntarily?"

"Utterly incapable."

"And also the soul which is under a tyrant (I am speaking of the soul taken as a whole) is least capable of doing what she desires; there is a gadfly which goads her, and she is full of trouble and remorse?"

"Certainly."

"And is the city which is under a tyrant rich or poor?"

"Poor."

"And the tyrannical soul must be always poor and insatiable?"

"True."

"And must not such a State and such a man be always full of fear?"

"Yes, indeed."

"Is there any State in which you will find more of lamentation and sorrow and groaning and pain?"
refashioned:
"That's a very fair proposal."

"Let's assume we ourselves are qualified judges who have encountered such a person. Then we can answer these questions directly."

"Agreed."

"Just remember to keep the parallel between individual and state in mind. Looking back and forth between them, tell me about their respective conditions."

"What do you mean?"

"Start with the state. Is a city governed by a tyrant free or enslaved?"

"No city could be more completely enslaved."

"Yet there are free men as well as masters in such a state?"

"Yes -- a few. But the people as a whole, especially the best among them, are miserably degraded and enslaved."

"Then if the man mirrors the state, the same must hold: his soul is full of baseness and vulgarity, the best parts of him are enslaved, and a small ruling element -- the worst and most deranged part -- commands everything."

"Inevitably."

"Would you say such a soul is the soul of a free man or a slave?"

"A slave, in my view."

"And a state enslaved under a tyrant is utterly incapable of voluntary action?"

"Utterly incapable."

"Likewise the tyrannized soul, taken as a whole, is least capable of doing what it truly wants. It's goaded by a stinging fly, full of turmoil and remorse."

"Certainly."

"Is the city under a tyrant rich or poor?"

"Poor."

"And the tyrannical soul -- always poor and insatiable?"

"True."

"And must not such a state and such a man be perpetually full of fear?"

"Yes, indeed."

"Can you find any state with more lamentation, sorrow, groaning, and pain?"
elegant: The tyrannical state is utterly enslaved, with the best citizens degraded. So too the tyrannical soul: its best parts are enslaved by the worst and maddest element. Such a soul is slavish, incapable of free action, goaded by internal torment, perpetually poor, insatiable, and full of fear.
summary: Socrates systematically parallels the tyrannical state and tyrannical soul: both are enslaved, with the best elements subjugated by the worst. Both are incapable of voluntary action, perpetually poor, insatiable, and consumed by fear, lamentation, and remorse.
distilled: The tyrannical soul, like the tyrannical state, is enslaved by its worst element -- perpetually poor, fearful, and incapable of freedom.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Systematic application of the city-soul analogy (Book II, 368c-369a) -- 'Soul of a slave' inverts the tyrant's apparent power -- he who seems most powerful is most enslaved -- 'Gadfly' (oistros) recalls Socrates' self-description as Athens' gadfly in the Apology (30e) -- The series of rapid questions and answers creates a mounting catalogue of misery -- Poverty, insatiability, fear, lamentation form the four marks of tyrannical existence -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 466b-468e on the tyrant who does what seems best but not what he truly wills -- 'The best elements enslaved' inverts Book IV's definition of justice as each part doing its proper work

narrative:
"Certainly not."

"And is there any man in whom you will find more of this sort of misery than in the tyrannical man, who is in a fury of passions and desires?"

"Impossible."

"Reflecting upon these and similar evils, you held the tyrannical State to be the most miserable of States?"

"And I was right," he said.

"Certainly," Socrates said. "And when you see the same evils in the tyrannical man, what do you say of him?"

"I say that he is by far the most miserable of all men."

"There," Socrates said, "I think that you are beginning to go wrong."

"What do you mean?"

"I do not think that he has as yet reached the utmost extreme of misery."

"Then who is more miserable?"

"One of whom I am about to speak."

"Who is that?"

"He who is of a tyrannical nature, and instead of leading a private life has been cursed with the further misfortune of being a public tyrant."

"From what has been said, I gather that you are right."

"Yes," Socrates replied, "but in this high argument you should be a little more certain, and should not conjecture only; for of all questions, this respecting good and evil is the greatest."

"Very true," he said.

"Let me then offer you an illustration, which may, I think, throw a light upon this subject."

"What is your illustration?"

"The case of rich individuals in cities who possess many slaves: from them you may form an idea of the tyrant's condition, for they both have slaves; the only difference is that he has more slaves."

"Yes, that is the difference."

"You know that they live securely and have nothing to apprehend from their servants?"

"What should they fear?"

"Nothing. But do you observe the reason of this?"

"Yes; the reason is, that the whole city is leagued together for the protection of each individual."

"Very true," Socrates said. "But imagine one of these owners, the master say of some fifty slaves, together with his family and property and slaves, carried off by a god into the wilderness, where there are no freemen to help him -- will he not be in an agony of fear lest he and his wife and children should be put to death by his slaves?"
refashioned:
"Certainly not."

"And can you find any individual more miserable than the tyrannical man, who is consumed by raging passions and desires?"

"Impossible."

"Considering all these evils, you judged the tyrannical state to be the most miserable of states?"

"And I was right."

"Certainly. And when you see the same evils in the tyrannical man, what do you say?"

"That he is by far the most miserable of all men."

"There I think you're beginning to go wrong."

"What do you mean?"

"I don't think he's reached the absolute extreme of misery yet."

"Who could be more miserable?"

"Someone I'm about to describe."

"Who?"

"A man with a tyrannical nature who, instead of remaining a private citizen, has the additional curse of actually becoming a public tyrant."

"Based on what you've said, I think you're right."

"Yes, but in an argument this important, you need to be more than merely persuaded -- you need certainty. The question of good and evil is the greatest question there is."

"Very true."

"Let me offer an illustration. Think about wealthy slaveholders in a city: they give you an idea of the tyrant's condition, since both have slaves -- the tyrant simply has more of them."

"Yes, that's the difference."

"These wealthy slaveholders live securely, with nothing to fear from their servants."

"What would they fear?"

"Nothing -- and do you see why?"

"Because the entire city collectively protects each individual citizen."

"Exactly. But now imagine one of these owners -- with fifty slaves, his family, and his property -- transported by a god into the wilderness where no free citizens can help him. Wouldn't he live in terror that his slaves would kill him and his wife and children?"
elegant: The tyrannical man is deeply miserable, but worse still is the tyrant who actually holds power. Like a slaveholder transported to a wilderness with no city to protect him, the public tyrant is surrounded by those he has enslaved -- and lives in constant terror of them.
summary: Socrates surprises Glaucon by arguing that the private tyrannical man is not yet the most miserable -- the public tyrant is worse. He illustrates with a slaveholder analogy: wealthy slaveholders feel safe because the city protects them, but imagine one transported to a wilderness alone with his slaves and no protection.
distilled: The public tyrant is more miserable than the private tyrannical man -- like a slaveholder stranded without the city's protection, surrounded by those he has enslaved.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Key distinction between tyrannical nature (private) and actual tyranny (public) -- the latter is worse -- 'Of all questions, this respecting good and evil is the greatest' underscores the stakes of the Republic's central argument -- The slaveholder analogy is vivid and politically charged in the context of Athenian slave-owning society -- 'Carried off by a god into the wilderness' is a thought experiment isolating the power relationship -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 469-474 on whether the tyrant or the tyrant's victim is worse off -- The argument moves from internal misery to the additional misery of political power

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "he will be in the utmost fear."

"The time has arrived when he will be compelled to flatter divers of his slaves, and make many promises to them of freedom and other things, much against his will -- he will have to cajole his own servants."

"Yes," he said, "that will be the only way of saving himself."

"And suppose the same god, who carried him away, to surround him with neighbours who will not suffer one man to be the master of another, and who, if they could catch the offender, would take his life?"

"His case will be still worse, if you suppose him to be everywhere surrounded and watched by enemies."
refashioned:
"Yes, he would be terrified."

"And now he'd be forced to flatter various slaves, making promises of freedom and other rewards -- completely against his will. He'd have to grovel before his own servants."

"Yes, that would be his only chance of survival."

"Now suppose the same god who transported him also surrounds him with neighbors who refuse to tolerate one person being master over another -- people who would kill any such offender on sight."

"His situation would be even worse -- completely surrounded and watched by enemies on all sides."
elegant: The isolated slaveholder must grovel before his own slaves to survive. Worse still if surrounded by neighbors who despise mastery itself -- he becomes a prisoner watched by enemies on every side.
summary: The stranded slaveholder is forced to flatter and bribe his own slaves. His situation worsens further if surrounded by neighbors hostile to slavery itself, leaving him trapped and watched by enemies on all sides.
distilled: The isolated master is forced to grovel before his own slaves, becoming a prisoner surrounded by enemies.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Continues the slaveholder thought experiment -- 'Flatter his own slaves' inverts the master-slave relationship, just as the tyrant's apparent power is actually servitude -- 'Neighbours who will not suffer one man to be master of another' may allude to democratic or egalitarian communities -- The escalating thought experiment parallels the tyrant's escalating isolation -- Cross-ref: Hiero (Xenophon) on the tyrant's inability to trust anyone -- 'Much against his will' emphasizes the loss of authentic agency -- the tyrant does not do what he truly wills (cf. Gorgias 466b-468e)

narrative: "And is not this the sort of prison in which the tyrant will be bound -- he who being by nature such as we have described, is full of all sorts of fears and lusts? His soul is dainty and greedy, and yet alone, of all men in the city, he is never allowed to go on a journey, or to see the things which other freemen desire to see, but he lives in his hole like a woman hidden in the house, and is jealous of any other citizen who goes into foreign parts and sees anything of interest."
refashioned: "And isn't this exactly the kind of prison the tyrant inhabits? By nature he is full of every kind of fear and craving. His soul is both fastidious and voracious. Yet he alone, of everyone in the city, can never travel or see the sights that other free people enjoy. He lives in his lair like a shut-in, envious of any citizen who travels abroad and experiences something worthwhile."
elegant: The tyrant is imprisoned by his own nature -- full of fears and lusts, unable to travel or experience what free people enjoy. He lives as a shut-in, envying the freedom of ordinary citizens.
summary: The tyrant lives in a prison of his own making, full of fears and lusts. Unlike free citizens, he can never travel or see the world, and envies those who can.
distilled: The tyrant is the most imprisoned person in his own city -- unable to travel, envying the freedom of ordinary citizens.
notes: Socrates speaking -- 'Lives in his hole like a woman hidden in the house' references the gendered seclusion of Athenian women in the gynaeceum -- The tyrant's immobility contrasts sharply with Greek ideals of travel and cosmopolitan experience -- 'Dainty and greedy' (tryphe kai pleonexia) combines luxury with acquisitiveness -- The tyrant's jealousy of ordinary citizens' freedom is deeply ironic -- Cross-ref: Xenophon's Hiero 1.11-2.1 on the tyrant's inability to enjoy festivals, travel, or public life -- The prison metaphor anticipates and inverts the Cave of Book VII: the tyrant voluntarily imprisons himself

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"And amid evils such as these will not he who is ill-governed in his own person -- the tyrannical man, I mean -- whom you just now decided to be the most miserable of all -- will not he be yet more miserable when, instead of leading a private life, he is constrained by fortune to be a public tyrant? He has to be master of others when he is not master of himself: he is like a diseased or paralytic man who is compelled to pass his life, not in retirement, but fighting and combating with other men."

"Yes," he said, "the similitude is most exact."

"Is not his case utterly miserable? And does not the actual tyrant lead a worse life than he whose life you determined to be the worst?"
refashioned:
"Very true."

"So consider this: the man who is badly governed within himself -- the tyrannical man you just declared the most miserable of all -- won't he be even more miserable when, instead of living privately, fate forces him to become an actual public tyrant? He must be master of others while unable to master himself. He's like a sick or paralyzed man compelled not to rest and recover, but to spend his life fighting and competing with other men."

"Yes, that comparison is exactly right."

"Isn't his situation utterly miserable? Doesn't the actual tyrant live a worse life than even the man you called the worst?"
elegant: The man who cannot govern himself is forced to govern others -- like a paralytic compelled to fight instead of rest. The public tyrant is even more miserable than the private tyrannical man.
summary: The public tyrant, unable to master himself yet forced to master others, is even more miserable than the private tyrannical man. Socrates compares him to a paralytic compelled to fight rather than rest.
distilled: The public tyrant must master others while unable to master himself -- a paralytic forced to fight.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Master of others when he is not master of himself' is the central paradox of tyranny -- 'Diseased or paralytic man' compelled to fight is a vivid analogy for the tyrannical condition -- power as affliction -- 'Constrained by fortune' (tyche) -- the tyrant doesn't choose his situation freely -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 466-468 on the powerlessness of tyrants who seem powerful -- The private/public tyrant distinction deepens the argument: political power adds misery to an already miserable soul -- Anticipates Aristotle's claim that the tyrant is unhappy (Nicomachean Ethics, Politics V)

narrative:
"Certainly."

"He who is the real tyrant, whatever men may think, is the real slave, and is obliged to practise the greatest adulation and servility, and to be the flatterer of the vilest of mankind. He has desires which he is utterly unable to satisfy, and has more wants than any one, and is truly poor, if you know how to inspect the whole soul of him: all his life long he is beset with fear and is full of convulsions, and distractions, even as the State which he resembles: and surely the resemblance holds?"
refashioned:
"Certainly."

"The real tyrant, whatever people may think, is the real slave. He must practice the most extreme flattery and servility, groveling before the vilest of people. He has desires he can never satisfy and more needs than anyone -- he is truly poor, if you know how to examine the whole soul. His entire life is besieged by fear, full of convulsions and agitation, just like the state he resembles. And surely the resemblance holds?"
elegant: The real tyrant is the real slave -- obliged to flatter the vilest people, tortured by insatiable desires, truly poor in soul, besieged by fear and inner convulsions, mirroring the wretched state he governs.
summary: The real tyrant is the real slave: forced to flatter the worst people, unable to satisfy his desires, truly poor in soul despite apparent wealth, and perpetually consumed by fear and inner turmoil -- a mirror of his wretched state.
distilled: The real tyrant is the real slave -- flattering the vile, insatiably poor in soul, and perpetually consumed by fear.

narrative:
"The decision will be easily given," he replied; "they shall be choruses coming on the stage, and I must judge them in the order in which they enter, by the criterion of virtue and vice, happiness and misery."

"Need we hire a herald, or shall I announce, that the son of Ariston (the best) has decided that the best and justest is also the happiest, and that this is he who is the most royal man and king over himself; and that the worst and most unjust man is also the most miserable, and that this is he who being the greatest tyrant of himself is also the greatest tyrant of his State?"

"Make the proclamation yourself," he said.

"And shall I add, 'whether seen or unseen by gods and men'?"

"Let the words be added."

"Then this," Socrates said, "will be our first proof; and there is another, which may also have some weight."

"What is that?"

"The second proof is derived from the nature of the soul: seeing that the individual soul, like the State, has been divided by us into three principles, the division may, I think, furnish a new demonstration."

"Of what nature?"

"It seems to me that to these three principles three pleasures correspond; also three desires and governing powers."
refashioned:
"The decision is easy," Glaucon replied. "Let them come on stage like competing choruses, and I'll judge them in order by the standard of virtue and vice, happiness and misery."

"Do we need to hire a herald, or shall I make the announcement myself? The son of Ariston -- whose name means 'the best' -- has decided that the best and most just man is also the happiest, and he is the one who is most kingly, most sovereign over himself. The worst and most unjust man is the most miserable, and he is the one who is the greatest tyrant of himself and of his state."

"Make the proclamation yourself."

"And shall I add: 'whether seen or unseen by gods and men'?"

"Add it."

"Then this is our first proof. But there's a second that may carry equal weight."

"What is it?"

"The second proof comes from the nature of the soul itself. We divided the soul, like the state, into three parts. This division can furnish a fresh demonstration."

"How so?"

"To these three parts of the soul correspond three distinct pleasures -- and three desires and governing principles."
elegant: First Proof concluded: the most just man, sovereign over himself, is the happiest; the tyrant of himself and his state is the most miserable -- whether seen or unseen by gods and men. The Second Proof begins from the soul's three parts, each with its own pleasure, desire, and governing principle.
summary: Glaucon's verdict concludes the First Proof: the just, kingly man is happiest and the tyrannical man most miserable, regardless of external appearances. Socrates then introduces the Second Proof, based on the tripartite soul's three corresponding pleasures.
distilled: First Proof: the just king is happiest, the self-tyrannizing tyrant most miserable, seen or unseen. Second Proof begins: three soul-parts yield three distinct pleasures.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Son of Ariston' is Glaucon himself (Plato's brother) -- ariston means 'best,' creating a pun -- 'Whether seen or unseen by gods and men' directly answers Book II's challenge (360b-362c) where Glaucon asked Socrates to prove justice good regardless of reputation -- FIRST PROOF CONCLUDED: justice = happiness, injustice = misery, based on city-soul parallel -- SECOND PROOF BEGINS: from the tripartite soul -- Cross-ref: Book IV (435b-441c) for the original division of the soul into reason, spirit, appetite -- The three pleasures correspond to the three soul-parts: philosophic pleasure (reason), honor (spirit), gain (appetite) -- The theatrical metaphor continues with 'choruses' and 'herald'

narrative:
"How do you mean?" he said.

"There is one principle with which, as we were saying, a man learns, another with which he is angry; the third, having many forms, has no special name, but is denoted by the general term appetitive, from the extraordinary strength and vehemence of the desires of eating and drinking and the other sensual appetites which are the main elements of it; also money-loving, because such desires are generally satisfied by the help of money."
refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"As we've established, there's one part of the soul with which we learn, another with which we feel anger and indignation. The third has many forms and no single name, but we call it the 'appetitive' part because of the extraordinary strength and intensity of its desires for eating, drinking, sex, and other bodily pleasures. We could also call it 'money-loving,' since money is the primary means of satisfying such desires."

narrative:
"That is true," he said.

"If we were to say that the loves and pleasures of this third part were concerned with gain, we should then be able to fall back on a single notion; and might truly and intelligibly describe this part of the soul as loving gain or money."

"I agree with you."

"Again, is not the passionate element wholly set on ruling and conquering and getting fame?"

"True."

"Suppose we call it the contentious or ambitious -- would the term be suitable?"

"Extremely suitable."

"On the other hand, every one sees that the principle of knowledge is wholly directed to the truth, and cares less than either of the others for gain or fame."

"Far less."

"'Lover of wisdom,' 'lover of knowledge,' are titles which we may fitly apply to that part of the soul?"

"Certainly."

"One principle prevails in the souls of one class of men, another in others, as may happen?"

"Yes."

"Then we may begin by assuming that there are three classes of men -- lovers of wisdom, lovers of honour, lovers of gain?"

"Exactly."

"And there are three kinds of pleasure, which are their several objects?"

"Very true."

"Now, if you examine the three classes of men, and ask of them in turn which of their lives is pleasantest, each will be found praising his own and depreciating that of others: the money-maker will contrast the vanity of honour or of learning if they bring no money with the solid advantages of gold and silver?"

"True," he said.

"And the lover of honour -- what will be his opinion? Will he not think that the pleasure of riches is vulgar, while the pleasure of learning, if it brings no distinction, is all smoke and nonsense to him?"

"Very true."

"And are we to suppose," Socrates said, "that the philosopher sets any value on other pleasures in comparison with the pleasure of knowing the truth, and in that pursuit abiding, ever learning, not so far indeed from the heaven of pleasure? Does he not call the other pleasures necessary, under the idea that if there were no necessity for them, he would rather not have them?"
refashioned:
"That's true."

"So if we say the third part's loves and pleasures are all concerned with gain, we have a clear unifying concept: it loves gain or money."

"Agreed."

"And the spirited part is entirely focused on dominance, conquest, and reputation?"

"True."

"We could call it the competitive or ambitious element?"

"Perfectly fitting."

"Meanwhile, everyone can see that the rational part is entirely directed toward truth, caring far less than the others for money or reputation."

"Far less."

"So we can call it the wisdom-loving or knowledge-loving part?"

"Certainly."

"And whichever part dominates determines the type of person -- so there are three classes: lovers of wisdom, lovers of honor, and lovers of gain?"

"Exactly."

"With three corresponding kinds of pleasure?"

"Yes."

"Now, if you ask each class which life is most pleasant, each will praise their own and dismiss the others. The money-maker will say that honor and learning are worthless unless they produce wealth."

"True."

"The honor-lover will call the pleasure of riches vulgar, and learning without distinction mere smoke and nonsense."

"Very true."

"And the philosopher? Does he value any pleasure compared with knowing truth and spending his life in continuous learning, close to the heaven of genuine pleasure? Doesn't he call the other pleasures merely 'necessary' -- things he'd happily do without if he could?"

The money-maker scorns unprofitable learning, the honor-lover dismisses vulgar wealth, and the philosopher calls all pleasures except truth merely necessary.
summary: Three classes of people correspond to the three soul-parts: lovers of wisdom, honor, and gain. Each class praises its own pleasure as the highest: the money-maker values only wealth, the honor-lover only distinction, and the philosopher only truth, regarding other pleasures as merely necessary.
distilled: Each of three human types -- wisdom-lover, honor-lover, gain-lover -- claims their own pleasure is supreme, setting up the question of who judges correctly.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Develops the Second Proof: which pleasure is genuinely highest? -- The three types map onto the tripartite soul: philosopher (reason), warrior (spirit), money-maker (appetite) -- Each type's self-praise creates an apparently irresolvable dispute -- the criterion for resolution will be experience, wisdom, and reason -- 'Not so far from the heaven of pleasure' elevates philosophic pleasure toward the divine -- 'Necessary pleasures' recalls Book VIII's distinction between necessary and unnecessary desires (558d-559d) -- Cross-ref: Philebus 20d-22e on the competition between pleasure and wisdom for the title of the Good -- The three-way dispute is a setup for the argument that only the philosopher can judge, since only he has experienced all three

narrative:
"There can be no doubt of that," he replied.

"Since, then, the pleasures of each class and the life of each are in dispute, and the question is not which life is more or less honourable, or better or worse, but which is the more pleasant or painless -- how shall we know who speaks truly?"

"I cannot myself tell," he said.

"Well, but what ought to be the criterion? Is any better than experience and wisdom and reason?"

"There cannot be a better," he said.

"Then," Socrates said, "reflect. Of the three individuals, which has the greatest experience of all the pleasures which we enumerated? Has the lover of gain, in learning the nature of essential truth, greater experience of the pleasure of knowledge than the philosopher has of the pleasure of gain?"

"The philosopher," he replied, "has greatly the advantage; for he has of necessity always known the taste of the other pleasures from his childhood upwards: but the lover of gain in all his experience has not of necessity tasted -- or, I should rather say, even had he desired, could hardly have tasted -- the sweetness of learning and knowing truth."

"Then the lover of wisdom has a great advantage over the lover of gain, for he has a double experience?"

"Yes, very great."

"Again, has he greater experience of the pleasures of honour, or the lover of honour of the pleasures of wisdom?"

"Nay," he said, "all three are honoured in proportion as they attain their object; for the rich man and the brave man and the wise man alike have their crowd of admirers, and as they all receive honour they all have experience of the pleasures of honour; but the delight which is to be found in the knowledge of true being is known to the philosopher only."

"His experience, then, will enable him to judge better than any one?"

"Far better."

"And he is the only one who has wisdom as well as experience?"

"Certainly."

"Further, the very faculty which is the instrument of judgment is not possessed by the covetous or ambitious man, but only by the philosopher?"
refashioned:
"No doubt about it."

"So the pleasures and lives of each type are in dispute, and the question isn't which life is more honorable or better, but which is genuinely more pleasant. How do we determine who's telling the truth?"

"I honestly don't know."

"What should the criterion be? Is there anything better than experience, wisdom, and reason?"

"Nothing better."

"Then consider this: of the three types, which has the broadest experience of all the pleasures we listed? Has the money-lover experienced more of the philosopher's pleasure in knowing truth than the philosopher has experienced of the money-lover's pleasure in gain?"

"The philosopher has a huge advantage. He's necessarily tasted the other pleasures since childhood -- everyone has. But the money-lover hasn't necessarily experienced, and even if he wanted to could hardly have tasted, the sweetness of learning and knowing truth."

"So the philosopher has a double advantage in experience over the money-lover?"

"A very great one."

"And compared to the honor-lover -- does the philosopher have more experience of honor's pleasures, or the honor-lover of wisdom's?"

"All three types receive honor in proportion to their success -- the rich man, the brave man, and the wise man each have their admirers. So all three know the pleasures of honor. But the delight found in knowing true reality is known to the philosopher alone."

"So his experience qualifies him to judge better than anyone?"

"Far better."

"And he alone possesses wisdom alongside experience?"

"Certainly."
