"Furthermore, the very instrument of judgment -- reason -- belongs not to the acquisitive or ambitious man, but only to the philosopher?"
elegant: The criterion for judging pleasures must be experience, wisdom, and reason. The philosopher has tasted all three pleasures since childhood, while the others have not tasted philosophic pleasure. Only the philosopher possesses all three qualifications for judgment: broadest experience, wisdom, and the instrument of reason itself.
summary: To resolve the dispute over which pleasure is greatest, Socrates proposes three criteria: experience, wisdom, and reason. The philosopher alone has experienced all three types of pleasure, possesses wisdom, and wields reason -- the very instrument of judgment. He is therefore the only qualified judge.
distilled: Only the philosopher can judge which pleasure is truest, because he alone has experienced all three and possesses both wisdom and reason.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The three criteria for judging pleasures: experience (empeiria), wisdom (phronesis), and reason (logos) -- The philosopher's 'double experience' argument: everyone experiences bodily and honor pleasures, but only philosophers experience intellectual pleasure -- 'Even had he desired, could hardly have tasted' -- philosophic pleasure requires long training, not just desire -- 'The instrument of judgment' (organon) is reason/logos itself -- Cross-ref: Philebus 11a-12b on the dispute between pleasure and wisdom -- Cross-ref: Book VII on the philosopher's return to the Cave, having seen all levels of reality -- This is the heart of the Second Proof: the philosopher as uniquely qualified arbiter

narrative:
"What faculty?"

"Reason, with whom, as we were saying, the decision ought to rest."

"Yes."

"And reasoning is peculiarly his instrument?"

"Certainly."

"If wealth and gain were the criterion, then the praise or blame of the lover of gain would surely be the most trustworthy?"

"Assuredly."

"Or if honour or victory or courage, in that case the judgement of the ambitious or pugnacious would be the truest?"

"Clearly."

"But since experience and wisdom and reason are the judges --"

"The only inference possible," he replied, "is that pleasures which are approved by the lover of wisdom and reason are the truest."

"And so we arrive at the result, that the pleasure of the intelligent part of the soul is the pleasantest of the three, and that he of us in whom this is the ruling principle has the pleasantest life."

"Unquestionably," he said, "the wise man speaks with authority when he approves of his own life."

"And what does the judge affirm to be the life which is next, and the pleasure which is next?"

"Clearly that of the soldier and lover of honour; who is nearer to himself than the money-maker."

"Last comes the lover of gain?"

"Very true," he said.

"Twice in succession, then, has the just man overthrown the unjust in this conflict; and now comes the third trial, which is dedicated to Olympian Zeus the saviour: a sage whispers in my ear that no pleasure except that of the wise is quite true and pure -- all others are a shadow only; and surely this will prove the greatest and most decisive of falls?"

"Yes, the greatest; but will you explain yourself?"

"I will work out the subject and you shall answer my questions."

"Proceed."

"Say, then, is not pleasure opposed to pain?"

"True."

"And there is a neutral state which is neither pleasure nor pain?"

"There is."

"A state which is intermediate, and a sort of repose of the soul about either -- that is what you mean?"

"Yes."

"You remember what people say when they are sick?"

"What do they say?"

"That after all nothing is pleasanter than health. But then they never knew this to be the greatest of pleasures until they were ill."
refashioned:
"Which faculty?"

"Reason -- which, as we said, ought to be the judge."

"Yes."

"And reasoning is the philosopher's special instrument?"

"Certainly."

"If wealth and gain were the criterion, the money-lover's judgment would be most trustworthy?"

"Of course."

"If honor, victory, or courage were the standard, the ambitious man's judgment would be truest?"

"Clearly."

"But since experience, wisdom, and reason are the proper judges --"

"Then the only possible conclusion is that the pleasures approved by the lover of wisdom and reason are the truest."

"So the pleasure of the rational part of the soul is the most genuine of the three, and whoever is governed by reason lives the most pleasant life."

"Without question -- the wise man speaks with authority when he endorses his own life."

"And which life does the judge rank second?"

"Obviously the soldier's and honor-lover's -- he's closer to the philosopher than the money-maker is."

"And last comes the money-lover?"

"Yes."

"So twice now the just man has defeated the unjust. Now comes the third round -- dedicated to Olympian Zeus the Savior. A wise voice whispers that no pleasure except the philosopher's is fully true and pure; all others are mere shadow. And surely this third fall will be the greatest and most decisive?"

"The greatest -- but explain what you mean."

"I'll work through it step by step. Answer my questions."

"Go ahead."

"Isn't pleasure opposed to pain?"

"Yes."

"And isn't there a neutral state that is neither pleasure nor pain?"

"There is."

"An intermediate state -- a kind of rest or equilibrium of the soul?"

"Yes."

"You know what people say when they're sick?"

"What?"

"That nothing is more pleasant than health. But they never realized health was the greatest pleasure until they fell ill."
elegant: Second Proof concluded: since experience, wisdom, and reason are the proper judges, the philosopher's pleasure is truest, the honor-lover's second, the money-lover's last. The just man defeats the unjust twice. Now begins the Third Proof: only philosophic pleasure is true and pure -- all others are shadow. What seems pleasant often is merely the cessation of pain.
summary: The Second Proof concludes: the philosopher's pleasure is judged truest by the only valid criteria. Socrates introduces the Third Proof -- dedicated to 'Olympian Zeus the Savior' -- arguing that most pleasures are merely shadows. He begins by examining the neutral state between pleasure and pain, noting that sick people mistake the relief of pain for the greatest pleasure.
distilled: The philosopher's pleasure wins by experience, wisdom, and reason; now the Third Proof begins: most pleasures are mere shadows of the cessation of pain.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- SECOND PROOF CONCLUDED: philosopher's pleasure > honor-lover's > money-lover's -- THIRD PROOF BEGINS: the argument from real vs. apparent pleasure -- 'Olympian Zeus the Savior' -- Greek custom of dedicating the third libation to Zeus Soter; the third proof is the decisive one -- 'A sage whispers' may allude to Orphic or Pythagorean wisdom traditions -- The pleasure/pain/neutral triad introduces a sophisticated hedonistic analysis -- 'Nothing pleasanter than health' illustrates confusing relief-from-pain with genuine pleasure -- Cross-ref: Philebus 31b-55c on true and false pleasures, the central parallel text -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 496c-497a on the relationship between pleasure and the cessation of pain -- This proof is the most philosophically original of the three

narrative:
"Yes, I know," he said.

"And when persons are suffering from acute pain, you must have heard them say that there is nothing pleasanter than to get rid of their pain?"

"I have."

"And there are many other cases of suffering in which the mere rest and cessation of pain, and not any positive enjoyment, is extolled by them as the greatest pleasure?"

"Yes," he said; "at the time they are pleased and well content to be at rest."

"Again, when pleasure ceases, that sort of rest or cessation will be painful?"

"Doubtless," he said.

"Then the intermediate state of rest will be pleasure and will also be pain?"

"So it would seem."

"But can that which is neither become both?"

"I should say not."

"And both pleasure and pain are motions of the soul, are they not?"

"Yes."

"But that which is neither was just now shown to be rest and not motion, and in a mean between them?"

"Yes."

"How, then, can we be right in supposing that the absence of pain is pleasure, or that the absence of pleasure is pain?"

"Impossible."

"This then is an appearance only and not a reality; that is to say, the rest is pleasure at the moment and in comparison of what is painful, and painful in comparison of what is pleasant; but all these representations, when tried by the test of true pleasure, are not real but a sort of imposition?"

"That is the inference."

"Look at the other class of pleasures which have no antecedent pains and you will no longer suppose, as you perhaps may at present, that pleasure is only the cessation of pain, or pain of pleasure."

"What are they," he said, "and where shall I find them?"

"There are many of them: take as an example the pleasures of smell, which are very great and have no antecedent pains; they come in a moment, and when they depart leave no pain behind them."

"Most true," he said.

"Let us not, then, be induced to believe that pure pleasure is the cessation of pain, or pain of pleasure."
refashioned:
"I know."

"And people suffering acute pain will say there's nothing more pleasant than to be rid of it?"

"I've heard them say it."

"In many cases of suffering, the mere cessation of pain -- not any positive enjoyment -- is praised as the greatest pleasure?"

"Yes, at the time they're perfectly content just to be at rest."

"But when pleasure ceases, that same state of rest feels painful?"

"No doubt."

"So the intermediate state of rest is sometimes called pleasure and sometimes pain?"

"It seems so."

"But can something that is neither pleasure nor pain actually be both?"

"It shouldn't be."

"Both pleasure and pain are movements of the soul, correct?"

"Yes."

"But we just agreed the neutral state is rest, not movement -- positioned between the two?"

"Yes."

"Then how can we be right to suppose the absence of pain is pleasure, or the absence of pleasure is pain?"

"We can't."

"So this is appearance, not reality. Rest seems pleasant only by contrast with pain, and painful only by contrast with pleasure. These impressions, tested against genuine pleasure, are not real but a kind of illusion."

"That follows."

"Now consider pleasures that have no prior pain at all, and you'll stop assuming pleasure is just the cessation of pain."

"What pleasures? Where do I find them?"

"There are many. The pleasures of smell, for instance -- they can be intense, they appear suddenly without any preceding pain, and when they disappear they leave no pain behind."

"Absolutely true."

"So let's not be deceived into thinking pure pleasure is merely the cessation of pain, or pain merely the cessation of pleasure."
elegant: Rest is neither pleasure nor pain but a neutral state. When we mistake the cessation of pain for pleasure, we are deceived by contrast -- it is appearance, not reality. True pleasures, like the pleasures of smell, arise without antecedent pain and leave none behind. Pure pleasure is not merely relief.
summary: Socrates demonstrates that the neutral state between pleasure and pain is neither but merely rest. Mistaking relief from pain for genuine pleasure is an illusion of contrast. True pleasures, like the pleasures of smell, arise without prior pain and prove that pure pleasure exists independently of pain's cessation.
distilled: Mistaking the cessation of pain for pleasure is an illusion; true pleasure, like the pleasure of smell, exists independently of any prior suffering.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Core of the Third Proof: the distinction between real and apparent pleasure -- The argument structure: (1) pleasure and pain are motions; (2) the neutral state is rest; (3) rest cannot be both; (4) therefore relief from pain is not true pleasure -- 'Pleasures of smell' as paradigm of pure, painless pleasure -- also discussed in Philebus 51b-52b -- 'A sort of imposition' (goeteia, enchantment/illusion) -- deceptive pleasures bewitch the soul -- Cross-ref: Philebus 42c-44d on false pleasures and the mixture of pleasure and pain -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 496c-497a on pleasure as replenishment -- This analysis anticipates modern distinctions between hedonic adaptation and genuine well-being

narrative:
"No."

"Still, the more numerous and violent pleasures which reach the soul through the body are generally of this sort -- they are reliefs of pain."

"That is true."

"And the anticipations of future pleasures and pains are of a like nature?"

"Yes."

"Shall I give you an illustration of them?"

"Let me hear."

"You would allow," Socrates said, "that there is in nature an upper and lower and middle region?"

"I should."

"And if a person were to go from the lower to the middle region, would he not imagine that he is going up; and he who is standing in the middle and sees whence he has come, would imagine that he is already in the upper region, if he has never seen the true upper world?"

"To be sure," he said; "how can he think otherwise?"

"But if he were taken back again he would imagine, and truly imagine, that he was descending?"

"No doubt."

"All that would arise out of his ignorance of the true upper and middle and lower regions?"
refashioned:
"No."

"Yet the most common and intense pleasures that reach the soul through the body are exactly this sort -- they're reliefs from pain."

"True."

"And our anticipations of future pleasures and pains work the same way?"

"Yes."

"Let me give you an analogy."

"Go ahead."

"You'd agree that in nature there's an upper region, a lower region, and a middle region?"

"Yes."

"If someone moved from the lower to the middle region, wouldn't he imagine he was ascending? And if he stood in the middle looking back at where he came from, wouldn't he believe he was already at the top -- if he had never actually seen the true upper region?"

"Of course -- how could he think otherwise?"

"But if he were brought back down, he would correctly believe he was descending?"

"No doubt."

"And all of this confusion would arise from his ignorance of the true upper, middle, and lower regions?"
elegant: Most bodily pleasures are merely relief from pain. Socrates illustrates with an analogy: someone moving from a lower to a middle region imagines he is at the top -- because he has never seen the true upper world. Without knowledge of genuine pleasure, we mistake the middle for the summit.
summary: Socrates observes that most bodily pleasures are reliefs from pain, then offers a spatial analogy: a person moving from a lower to middle region would mistake it for the top if he had never seen the true upper world -- just as we mistake relief from pain for genuine pleasure.
distilled: Without experience of true pleasure, we mistake relief from pain for the summit -- like someone who has never seen the upper world calling the middle region the top.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The three-region analogy (upper/middle/lower) maps onto: true pleasure / neutral state / pain -- 'Never seen the true upper world' echoes the Cave allegory (Book VII, 514a-517a) -- those who have never known genuine pleasure mistake relief for the highest good -- 'Anticipations of future pleasures' extends the analysis to expectation and imagination, not just present sensation -- Cross-ref: Philebus 36a-40e on true and false anticipatory pleasures -- The spatial metaphor is characteristic of Plato's use of vertical imagery for ontological and moral hierarchy -- This analogy prepares for the ontological argument about degrees of reality and pleasure

narrative:
"Yes."

"Then can you wonder that persons who are inexperienced in the truth, as they have wrong ideas about many other things, should also have wrong ideas about pleasure and pain and the intermediate state; so that when they are only being drawn towards the painful they feel pain and think the pain which they experience to be real, and in like manner, when drawn away from pain to the neutral or intermediate state, they firmly believe that they have reached the goal of satiety and pleasure; they, not knowing pleasure, err in contrasting pain with the absence of pain, which is like contrasting black with grey instead of white -- can you wonder, I say, at this?"
refashioned:
"Yes."

"Then is it any wonder that people inexperienced in truth -- who have wrong ideas about so many things -- also have wrong ideas about pleasure and pain? When they're being pulled toward pain, they feel it and think it's real. When drawn away from pain to the neutral state, they're convinced they've reached genuine satisfaction and pleasure. Not knowing true pleasure, they make the mistake of contrasting pain with the mere absence of pain -- which is like contrasting black with grey instead of with white. Can you really be surprised?"
elegant: Those inexperienced in truth mistake the neutral state for genuine pleasure, contrasting pain with its mere absence -- like contrasting black with grey instead of white. Without knowledge of true pleasure, they can never recognize their error.
summary: People inexperienced in truth mistake the absence of pain for real pleasure, just as someone might contrast black with grey rather than with white. Without knowing genuine pleasure, they cannot recognize their error.
distilled: Those who have never known true pleasure mistake its absence of pain for the real thing -- confusing grey with white.
notes: Socrates speaking -- The black/grey/white analogy crystallizes the Third Proof's argument: most people's 'pleasures' are grey (neutral) mistaken for white (true pleasure) -- 'Inexperienced in truth' connects the pleasure argument to the Republic's broader epistemology -- ignorance about pleasure parallels ignorance about the Good -- 'Goal of satiety' (plesmone) -- mere filling/satisfaction vs. genuine fulfillment -- Cross-ref: Book VII's Cave allegory -- the prisoners mistake shadows for reality, as people mistake relief for pleasure -- Cross-ref: Philebus 41e-42c on false judgments of pleasure -- The argument has implications for utilitarian ethics: if most people can't identify genuine pleasure, democratic hedonism is unreliable

narrative:
"No, indeed; I should be much more disposed to wonder at the opposite."

"Look at the matter thus: Hunger, thirst, and the like, are inanitions of the bodily state?"

"Yes."

"And ignorance and folly are inanitions of the soul?"

"True."

"And food and wisdom are the corresponding satisfactions of either?"

"Certainly."

"And is the satisfaction derived from that which has less or from that which has more existence the truer?"
refashioned:
"No -- I'd be more surprised if it were otherwise."

"Now look at it this way: hunger, thirst, and similar states are deficiencies of the body?"

"Yes."

"And ignorance and foolishness are deficiencies of the soul?"

"True."

"And food satisfies bodily deficiency, while wisdom satisfies the soul's deficiency?"

"Certainly."

"And which satisfaction is truer -- the one derived from what has less reality, or from what has more?"
elegant: Hunger is a deficiency of the body; ignorance is a deficiency of the soul. Food fills one, wisdom fills the other. The satisfaction from what has more reality -- wisdom -- must be the truer satisfaction.
summary: Socrates draws a parallel between bodily and psychic deficiency: hunger is to the body what ignorance is to the soul, and food is to hunger what wisdom is to ignorance. The satisfaction derived from what has more reality (wisdom) must be truer.
distilled: Wisdom satisfies the soul's hunger as food satisfies the body's -- but wisdom, having more reality, provides the truer satisfaction.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Inanitions' (kenoseis, emptyings/depletions) -- Plato's model of desire as lack/deficiency to be filled, developed in the Symposium (200a-201c) -- The body/soul parallel: food:body :: wisdom:soul -- but with an ontological hierarchy -- 'More or less existence' introduces the Forms-based ontological argument -- objects of knowledge have more Being than objects of appetite -- Cross-ref: Symposium 200a-201c on desire as lack -- Cross-ref: Philebus 31b-32b on pleasure as restoration of natural state -- This passage pivots the Third Proof from phenomenology (how pleasures appear) to ontology (what pleasures really are)

narrative:
"Clearly, from that which has more."

"What classes of things have a greater share of pure existence in your judgment -- those of which food and drink and condiments and all kinds of sustenance are examples, or the class which contains true opinion and knowledge and mind and all the different kinds of virtue? Put the question in this way: Which has a more pure being -- that which is concerned with the invariable, the immortal, and the true, and is of such a nature, and is found in such natures; or that which is concerned with and found in the variable and mortal, and is itself variable and mortal?"
refashioned:
"Clearly, from what has more reality."

"Then which class of things has a greater share of pure existence? The class that includes food, drink, seasonings, and all physical sustenance? Or the class that includes true opinion, knowledge, mind, and every kind of virtue? Put it this way: which has purer being -- what is concerned with the unchanging, immortal, and true, and is of that nature and found in such natures? Or what is concerned with the changing and mortal, and is itself changing and mortal?"
elegant: Which has more pure being: food and drink, which are variable and mortal, or knowledge and virtue, which are concerned with the unchanging, immortal, and true? The answer determines which satisfaction is more real.
summary: Socrates asks which has greater reality: physical sustenance (food, drink) or intellectual sustenance (knowledge, virtue, true opinion). The things concerned with the unchanging and immortal have purer being than those concerned with the variable and mortal.
distilled: Knowledge and virtue, concerned with the unchanging and immortal, have more pure being than food and drink -- and therefore provide truer satisfaction.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The ontological argument: objects of the soul (knowledge, virtue) participate more in Being than objects of the body (food, drink) -- 'Invariable, immortal, and true' describes the Forms of Book V-VII -- 'Variable and mortal' describes the sensible world -- The argument structure: (1) truer satisfaction comes from what has more being; (2) knowledge has more being than food; (3) therefore philosophic pleasure is truer -- Cross-ref: Book V (475e-480a) on knowledge vs. opinion and their objects -- Cross-ref: Book VI (508d-509b) on the Form of the Good as source of being and truth -- Cross-ref: Philebus 58a-59d on the ontological hierarchy of pleasures

narrative:
"Far purer," he replied, "is the being of that which is concerned with the invariable."

"And does the essence of the invariable partake of knowledge in the same degree as of essence?"

"Yes, of knowledge in the same degree."

"And of truth in the same degree?"

"Yes."

"And, conversely, that which has less of truth will also have less of essence?"

"Necessarily."

"Then, in general, those kinds of things which are in the service of the body have less of truth and essence than those which are in the service of the soul?"

"Far less."

"And has not the body itself less of truth and essence than the soul?"

"Yes."

"What is filled with more real existence, and actually has a more real existence, is more really filled than that which is filled with less real existence and is less real?"

"Of course."

"And if there be a pleasure in being filled with that which is according to nature, that which is more really filled with more real being will more really and truly enjoy true pleasure; whereas that which participates in less real being will be less truly and surely satisfied, and will participate in an illusory and less real pleasure?"
refashioned:
"Far purer is the being of what is concerned with the unchanging."

"And the essence of the unchanging partakes of knowledge to the same degree as it partakes of being?"

"Yes, equally."

"And of truth to the same degree?"

"Yes."

"And conversely, what has less truth also has less being?"

"Necessarily."

"So in general, things that serve the body have less truth and less being than things that serve the soul?"

"Far less."

"And the body itself has less truth and being than the soul?"

"Yes."

"Then what is filled with more real being, and itself has more real being, is more genuinely filled than what is filled with less real being?"

"Of course."

"And if pleasure consists in being filled with what is natural to you, then being filled with more real being produces more real, genuine pleasure -- while being filled with less real being produces a less true, more illusory pleasure?"
elegant: Things that serve the soul have more truth and being than things that serve the body. The body itself has less being than the soul. Therefore, being filled with knowledge (more real) produces truer pleasure than being filled with food (less real). Bodily pleasures are comparatively illusory.
summary: Socrates establishes an ontological hierarchy: things serving the soul have more truth and being than those serving the body, and the soul itself has more being than the body. Therefore, being filled with what is more real (knowledge) produces truer pleasure than being filled with what is less real (food).
distilled: Being filled with what has more being (knowledge) produces genuinely truer pleasure than being filled with what has less being (food).
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The argument from degrees of reality: more real objects produce more real satisfactions -- 'Filled with more real existence' (pleroumena mallon ontos) -- pleasure as plēsis (filling) with Being itself -- The chain: being = truth = knowledge; less being = less truth = less knowledge -- 'Illusory and less real pleasure' -- bodily pleasures are ontologically deficient, not merely subjectively inferior -- Cross-ref: Book VI's Divided Line (509d-511e) maps levels of reality onto levels of cognition -- Cross-ref: Philebus 52c-53c on pure vs. mixed pleasures and their relation to truth -- The argument has Parmenidean roots: Being is more real than Becoming -- This is the ontological foundation for the claim that philosophic pleasure is truest

narrative:
"Unquestionably."

"Those then who know not wisdom and virtue, and are always busy with gluttony and sensuality, go down and up again as far as the mean; and in this region they move at random throughout life, but they never pass into the true upper world; thither they neither look, nor do they ever find their way, neither are they truly filled with true being, nor do they taste of pure and abiding pleasure. Like cattle, with their eyes always looking down and their heads stooping to the earth, that is, to the dining-table, they fatten and feed and breed, and, in their excessive love of these delights, they kick and butt at one another with horns and hoofs which are made of iron; and they kill one another by reason of their insatiable lust. For they fill themselves with that which is not substantial, and the part of themselves which they fill is also unsubstantial and incontinent."
refashioned:
"Unquestionably."

"So those who never know wisdom and virtue, who are perpetually occupied with gluttony and sensuality -- they oscillate between pain and the neutral midpoint, wandering randomly through life in that range. They never ascend to the true upper region. They never look upward, never find the way there, never fill themselves with genuine being, never taste pure and lasting pleasure. Like cattle with eyes fixed downward, heads bent toward the ground -- that is, toward the dinner table -- they gorge themselves, breed, and grow fat. In their excessive greed for these delights, they kick and gore each other with iron hooves and horns, and they kill each other out of insatiable lust. For they fill themselves with what lacks substance, and the part of themselves they fill is equally insubstantial and without self-control."
elegant: Those who never know wisdom oscillate between pain and the neutral midpoint, never tasting true pleasure. Like cattle with eyes fixed on the feeding trough, they gorge, breed, and kill each other with iron horns out of insatiable lust -- filling their insubstantial selves with insubstantial things.
summary: Those ignorant of wisdom and virtue oscillate between pain and the neutral state, never reaching true pleasure. Socrates compares them to cattle: eyes fixed on the feeding trough, they gorge and breed, fighting and killing each other with iron horns out of insatiable lust, filling their unsubstantial selves with unsubstantial things.
distilled: Those who never know wisdom live like cattle -- eyes fixed on the feeding trough, fighting with iron horns, filling their empty selves with empty things.
notes: Socrates speaking -- One of the Republic's most vivid and brutal images -- 'Like cattle' (boskemata) reduces the appetitive life to animal existence -- 'Iron horns and hoofs' intensifies the natural into the unnatural and destructive -- iron suggests weaponized appetite -- 'Go down and up again as far as the mean' -- they oscillate between pain and neutral, never reaching genuine pleasure -- The 'true upper world' is the realm of Forms and genuine knowledge -- 'Insatiable lust' (aplestia) -- the defining vice of the appetitive soul -- Cross-ref: Book VII's Cave (514a-517a) -- the cattle are the unphilosophical prisoners -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 81e-82b on souls reincarnated as animals matching their character -- Cross-ref: Philebus 67b on the life of pleasure without mind being the life of an oyster

narrative:
"Verily, Socrates," said Glaucon, "you describe the life of the many like an oracle."

"Their pleasures are mixed with pains -- how can they be otherwise? For they are mere shadows and pictures of the true, and are coloured by contrast, which exaggerates both light and shade, and so they implant in the minds of fools insane desires of themselves; and they are fought about as Stesichorus says that the Greeks fought about the shadow of Helen at Troy in ignorance of the truth."

"Something of that sort must inevitably happen."

"And must not the like happen with the spirited or passionate element of the soul? Will not the passionate man who carries his passion into action, be in the like case, whether he is envious and ambitious, or violent and contentious, or angry and discontented, if he be seeking to attain honour and victory and the satisfaction of his anger without reason or sense?"

"Yes," he said, "the same will happen with the spirited element also."
refashioned:
"Truly, Socrates," said Glaucon, "you describe the life of the masses like an oracle."

"Their pleasures are inevitably mixed with pain. They are mere shadows and pictures of genuine pleasure, colored by contrast in a way that exaggerates both light and dark. This implants insane cravings in the minds of fools. People fight over these shadow-pleasures just as the Greeks, according to Stesichorus, fought over the phantom of Helen at Troy, ignorant of the truth."

"Something like that is bound to happen."

"And doesn't the same apply to the spirited part of the soul? The passionate man who acts on his passions -- whether from envy and ambition, violence and combativeness, or anger and resentment -- seeking honor, victory, or the satisfaction of his anger without reason or judgment: isn't he in the same position?"

"Yes, the same thing happens with the spirited element too."
elegant: The masses' pleasures are shadows colored by contrast, implanting insane desires. People fight over them as the Greeks fought over the phantom of Helen at Troy. The same applies to the spirited soul: honor and victory pursued without reason yield only shadow-pleasures.
summary: Glaucon calls Socrates' description oracular. Socrates explains that the masses' pleasures are shadows colored by contrast, generating insane cravings -- like the Greeks who fought over the phantom of Helen at Troy. The same illusion afflicts the spirited soul: honor pursued without reason produces only shadow-pleasure.
distilled: The masses fight over shadow-pleasures as the Greeks fought over Helen's phantom; the spirited soul's honor-seeking without reason is equally illusory.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Like an oracle' (mantikos) -- Glaucon acknowledges the prophetic force of the description -- The Helen reference is to Stesichorus' Palinode, which claimed the real Helen never went to Troy -- only her phantom (eidolon) did. Euripides dramatized this in his Helen -- 'Shadows and pictures of the true' connects pleasure to the Cave's shadows (Book VII) and the artist's imitations (Book X) -- 'Coloured by contrast' explains why relief-pleasures seem intense despite being unreal -- The argument extends from appetitive to spirited pleasures: honor pursued without reason is equally illusory -- Cross-ref: Book X (598b-602b) on imitation at three removes from truth -- Cross-ref: Philebus 40a-41a on false pleasures as paintings in the soul

narrative: "Then may we not confidently assert that the lovers of money and honour, when they seek their pleasures under the guidance and in the company of reason and knowledge, and pursue after and win the pleasures which wisdom shows them, will also have the truest pleasures in the highest degree which is attainable to them, inasmuch as they follow truth; and they will have the pleasures which are natural to them, if that which is best for each one is also most natural to him?"
refashioned: "Then can we not confidently say this: when lovers of money and honor pursue their pleasures under the guidance of reason and knowledge -- seeking and winning the pleasures that wisdom identifies as genuine -- they will enjoy the truest pleasures available to them, since they are following truth? And these will be their most natural pleasures, if what is best for each person is also what is most natural to them."
elegant: When the money-loving and honor-loving parts pursue pleasure under reason's guidance, they achieve their truest and most natural pleasures. What is best for each part is what is most natural to it -- and reason alone can identify what that is.
summary: Socrates argues that even the lower soul-parts achieve their truest and most natural pleasures when guided by reason and knowledge. What is best for each part is most natural to it, and only wisdom can identify genuine pleasure.
distilled: Even appetitive and spirited pleasures become truest and most natural when pursued under the guidance of reason.
notes: Socrates speaking -- Key integrative claim: the three soul-parts are not at war in the just soul; each achieves its best pleasure when governed by reason -- 'Best for each is most natural' connects ethics to nature (phusis) -- what is truly good is what accords with one's nature -- This is not the suppression of appetite and spirit but their fulfillment under rational governance -- Cross-ref: Book IV (441d-442a) on justice as each part doing its own work -- when reason governs, all parts are satisfied -- Cross-ref: Philebus 63b-64a on the mixed life of pleasure and wisdom as the genuine good -- The passage reconciles the hierarchy of pleasures with the legitimacy of non-philosophical pleasures properly governed

narrative:
"Yes, certainly; the best is the most natural."

"And when the whole soul follows the philosophical principle, and there is no division, the several parts are just, and do each of them their own business, and enjoy severally the best and truest pleasures of which they are capable?"

"Exactly."

"But when either of the two other principles prevails, it fails in attaining its own pleasure, and compels the rest to pursue after a pleasure which is a shadow only and which is not their own?"

"True."

"And the greater the interval which separates them from philosophy and reason, the more strange and illusive will be the pleasure?"

"Yes."

"And is not that farthest from reason which is at the greatest distance from law and order?"

"Clearly."

"And the lustful and tyrannical desires are, as we saw, at the greatest distance?"

"Yes."

"And the royal and orderly desires are nearest?"

"Yes."

"Then the tyrant will live at the greatest distance from true or natural pleasure, and the king at the least?"

"Certainly."

"But if so, the tyrant will live most unpleasantly, and the king most pleasantly?"

"Inevitably."

"Would you know the measure of the interval which separates them?"

"Will you tell me?"

"There appear to be three pleasures, one genuine and two spurious: now the transgression of the tyrant reaches a point beyond the spurious; he has run away from the region of law and reason, and taken up his abode with certain slave pleasures which are his satellites, and the measure of his inferiority can only be expressed in a figure."

"How do you mean?"

"I assume," Socrates said, "that the tyrant is in the third place from the oligarch; the democrat was in the middle?"

"Yes."

"And if there is truth in what has preceded, he will be wedded to an image of pleasure which is thrice removed as to truth from the pleasure of the oligarch?"

"He will."

"And the oligarch is third from the royal; since we count as one royal and aristocratical?"

"Yes, he is third."

"Then the tyrant is removed from true pleasure by the space of a number which is three times three?"
refashioned:
"Yes, what's best is most natural."

"And when the whole soul follows reason's lead without internal conflict, each part does its own work and enjoys the best and truest pleasure it's capable of?"

"Exactly."

"But when either of the other two parts takes over, it fails to achieve even its own genuine pleasure, and forces the others to chase pleasures that are merely shadows -- not truly their own?"

"True."

"And the further a pleasure is from philosophy and reason, the stranger and more illusory it becomes?"

"Yes."

"And what's farthest from reason is farthest from law and order?"

"Clearly."

"And the lustful, tyrannical desires are the farthest, as we established?"

"Yes."

"While the kingly, orderly desires are the nearest?"

"Yes."

"So the tyrant lives at the greatest distance from true pleasure, and the king at the least?"

"Certainly."

"Which means the tyrant lives most unpleasantly, the king most pleasantly?"

"Inevitably."

"Would you like to know the measure of the distance between them?"

"Tell me."

"There are three pleasures -- one genuine and two spurious. The tyrant transgresses even beyond the spurious. He has fled the realm of law and reason to live among slave-pleasures. His inferiority can only be expressed mathematically."

"What do you mean?"

"The tyrant is three places removed from the oligarch, with the democrat in between."

"Yes."

"So his pleasure is an image three times removed in truth from the oligarch's?"

"It is."

"And the oligarch is third from the philosopher-king, counting royal and aristocratic as one?"

"Yes, third."

"Then the tyrant is removed from true pleasure by three times three?"
elegant: When reason governs the soul, all parts enjoy their truest pleasures. The further from reason, the more illusory the pleasure. The tyrant, farthest from law and reason, lives at the greatest distance from true pleasure. Mathematically, the tyrant is removed from the king's pleasure by three times three -- nine degrees of separation.
summary: When reason rules, all soul-parts enjoy their truest pleasures. When appetite or spirit rules instead, all parts are compelled toward shadow-pleasures. The tyrant's distance from genuine pleasure can be calculated: three places from the oligarch, who is three from the king, yielding a separation of nine.
distilled: The tyrant is nine steps removed from the king's true pleasure, living among slave-pleasures at the furthest distance from reason and law.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The mathematical calculation of pleasure begins: tyrant is 3 x 3 = 9 steps from true pleasure -- 'Slave pleasures' (doulikai hedonai) -- the tyrant's pleasures enslave rather than liberate -- 'One genuine and two spurious' pleasures correspond to the three soul-parts -- 'Royal and aristocratical counted as one' simplifies the five regimes for calculation -- The five-regime ranking: royal > timocratic > oligarchic > democratic > tyrannical -- The counting: oligarch is 3rd from king; tyrant is 3rd from oligarch (with democrat between) -- Cross-ref: Book VIII's five regime types and their degeneration -- The mathematical argument anticipates the 729 calculation in 9.42 -- 'Run away from the region of law and reason' echoes the lawless appetites of 9.1-9.2

narrative:
"Manifestly."

"The shadow then of tyrannical pleasure determined by the number of length will be a plane figure."

"Certainly."

"And if you raise the power and make the plane a solid, there is no difficulty in seeing how vast is the interval by which the tyrant is parted from the king."

"Yes; the arithmetician will easily do the sum."

"Or if some person begins at the other end and measures the interval by which the king is parted from the tyrant in truth of pleasure, he will find him, when the multiplication is complete, living 729 times more pleasantly, and the tyrant more painfully by this same interval."

"What a wonderful calculation! And how enormous is the distance which separates the just from the unjust in regard to pleasure and pain!"

"Yet a true calculation," Socrates said, "and a number which nearly concerns human life, if human beings are concerned with days and nights and months and years."

"Yes," he said, "human life is certainly concerned with them."

"Then if the good and just man be thus superior in pleasure to the evil and unjust, his superiority will be infinitely greater in propriety of life and in beauty and virtue?"

"Immeasurably greater."

"Well," Socrates said, "and now having arrived at this stage of the argument, we may revert to the words which brought us hither: Was not some one saying that injustice was a gain to the perfectly unjust who was reputed to be just?"

"Yes, that was said."

"Now then, having determined the power and quality of justice and injustice, let us have a little conversation with him."

"What shall we say to him?"

"Let us make an image of the soul, that he may have his own words presented before his eyes."

"Of what sort?"

"An ideal image of the soul, like the composite creations of ancient mythology, such as the Chimera or Scylla or Cerberus, and there are many others in which two or more different natures are said to grow into one."

"There are said to have been such unions."

"Then do you now model the form of a multitudinous, many-headed monster, having a ring of heads of all manner of beasts, tame and wild, which he is able to generate and metamorphose at will."
refashioned:
"Obviously."

"The shadow of tyrannical pleasure, measured in linear terms, gives us a plane figure -- nine squared: eighty-one."

"Certainly."

"And if you raise it to a solid -- cubing the number -- you can see how vast the interval is between tyrant and king."

"Any mathematician can do the calculation."

"And if someone measures from the other direction -- how much more pleasantly the king lives than the tyrant -- the multiplication yields 729 times. The king lives 729 times more pleasantly; the tyrant 729 times more painfully."

"What an extraordinary calculation! The distance between the just and the unjust in pleasure and pain is staggering!"

"Yet it's a true calculation, and the number has a connection to human life -- since human beings are concerned with days and nights and months and years."

"Human life is certainly bound up with those."

"And if the just man is this much superior in pleasure, his superiority in dignity of life, beauty, and virtue must be immeasurably greater?"

"Immeasurably."

"Good. Now that we've reached this point, let's return to what started this whole inquiry. Someone claimed that injustice is profitable for the perfectly unjust man who is reputed to be just."

"Yes, that was the claim."

"Having now determined the power and quality of justice and injustice, let's have a word with that person."

"What shall we say?"

"Let's construct an image of the soul so he can see his own argument illustrated before his eyes."

"What kind of image?"

"A composite image, like the mythological creatures of old -- the Chimera, Scylla, Cerberus -- where two or more different natures grow into one."

"Such creatures are described in the myths."

"Now picture a many-headed beast with a ring of heads -- all kinds of animals, tame and wild -- that it can generate and transform at will."
elegant: The king lives 729 times more pleasantly than the tyrant -- a number connected to the days and nights of human life. If the just man's superiority in pleasure is this vast, his superiority in virtue is immeasurable. Now Socrates constructs an image of the soul: a composite creature like the Chimera, with a many-headed beast representing the appetites.
summary: The mathematical proof concludes: the philosopher-king lives 729 times more pleasantly than the tyrant. Socrates then returns to the original challenge from Book II and proposes an image of the soul as a composite mythological creature -- a many-headed beast (appetite), to which a lion and a man will be added.
distilled: The king lives 729 times more pleasantly than the tyrant; now Socrates images the soul as a composite beast to show why justice benefits and injustice harms.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 729 = 9 cubed (9 x 9 x 9), or equivalently 3 to the 6th power -- 729 also equals the number of days and nights in a year (365 days x 2 = 730, approximately 729) -- This connects cosmic time to the measure of pleasure -- 'Revert to the words which brought us hither' returns to Book II's challenge (Glaucon at 360e-362c, Adeimantus at 362d-367e) -- The Chimera, Scylla, and Cerberus are composite mythological creatures combining multiple animal natures -- The many-headed beast = appetite (hydra-like, many desires); the lion = spirit; the man = reason -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-b charioteer allegory as a parallel soul-image -- THREE PROOFS NOW COMPLETE; the image of the soul is the culminating argument

narrative:
"You suppose marvellous powers in the artist; but, as language is more pliable than wax or any similar substance, let there be such a model as you propose."

"Suppose now that you make a second form as of a lion, and a third of a man, the second smaller than the first, and the third smaller than the second."

"That," he said, "is an easier task; and I have made them as you say."

"And now join them, and let the three grow into one."

"That has been accomplished."

"Next fashion the outside of them into a single image, as of a man, so that he who is not able to look within, and sees only the outer hull, may believe the beast to be a single human creature."

"I have done so," he said."
refashioned:
"That requires quite an artistic imagination -- but since language is more pliable than wax, let's create the model you describe."

"Now make a second form: a lion. And a third: a man. The lion should be smaller than the many-headed beast, and the man smaller than the lion."

"That's easier. Done."

"Now join all three and let them grow into a single being."

"Done."

"Finally, wrap the outside in the form of a single human figure, so that anyone who can't see inside -- who sees only the outer shell -- would believe it to be a single human being."

"Done."
elegant: The soul's image: a many-headed beast (appetite), a lion (spirit), and a man (reason), joined into one and wrapped in a human exterior. Those who see only the outside believe it is simply a person.
summary: Socrates completes the composite image of the soul: a many-headed beast (appetite), a lion (spirit), and a man (reason), fused together and enclosed in a human exterior. To outside observers, it appears to be a single human being.
distilled: The soul is a beast, a lion, and a man fused inside a human shell -- invisible to those who see only the exterior.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- The three components map exactly to the tripartite soul: many-headed beast = appetitive (largest), lion = spirited (middle), man = rational (smallest) -- Size hierarchy (beast > lion > man) reflects the relative strength of each part in most people -- 'Language is more pliable than wax' -- a reflexive comment on the power of philosophical imagery -- 'He who is not able to look within' -- the unphilosophical observer who judges by appearances -- the Republic's recurring theme -- The human exterior is the appearance of unity that masks internal conflict -- Cross-ref: Phaedrus 246a-b chariot allegory; Republic Book IV (435b-441c) tripartite soul -- The image will now be used to demonstrate that injustice means feeding the beast while starving the man

narrative: "And now, to him who maintains that it is profitable for the human creature to be unjust, and unprofitable to be just, let us reply that, if he be right, it is profitable for this creature to feast the multitudinous monster and strengthen the lion and the lion-like qualities, but to starve and weaken the man, who is consequently liable to be dragged about at the mercy of either of the other two; and he is not to attempt to familiarize or harmonize them with one another -- he ought rather to suffer them to fight and bite and devour one another."
refashioned: "Now let's respond to anyone who claims injustice profits a person while justice does not. If he's right, then it's profitable to feast the many-headed monster and strengthen the lion -- but to starve and weaken the inner man, leaving him at the mercy of the other two, dragged about wherever they pull him. According to this view, one shouldn't try to harmonize or familiarize the three parts with each other. Instead, one should let them fight, bite, and devour one another."
elegant: If injustice is profitable, then one should feast the beast, strengthen the lion, and starve the man within -- letting all three fight and devour each other rather than live in harmony.
summary: Socrates uses the composite image to expose the absurdity of defending injustice: it amounts to feasting the beast and lion while starving the rational man, then letting all three parts fight and devour each other instead of achieving harmony.
distilled: Defending injustice means feeding the beast and starving reason -- letting the soul's parts fight and devour each other.
notes: Socrates speaking -- The reductio ad absurdum of injustice using the soul-image: injustice = feeding appetite (beast) and spirit (lion) while starving reason (man) -- 'Dragged about at the mercy' of appetite and spirit describes internal tyranny -- 'Fight and bite and devour one another' depicts injustice as psychic civil war -- Cross-ref: Book IV (444b) on injustice as disease and civil war of the soul -- The image makes visceral what the abstract argument established: injustice is self-destruction -- 'Not to attempt to familiarize or harmonize' is the opposite of justice, which was defined as each part doing its own work in harmony (Book IV, 443c-444a)

narrative:
"Certainly," he said; "that is what the approver of injustice says."

"To him the supporter of justice makes answer that he should ever so speak and act as to give the man within him in some way or other the most complete mastery over the entire human creature. He should watch over the many-headed monster like a good husbandman, fostering and cultivating the gentle qualities, and preventing the wild ones from growing; he should be making the lion-heart his ally, and in common care of them all should be uniting the several parts with one another and with himself."

"Yes," he said, "that is quite what the maintainer of justice says."

"And so from every point of view, whether of pleasure, honour, or advantage, the approver of justice is right and speaks the truth, and the disapprover is wrong and false and ignorant."

"Yes, from every point of view."

"Come, now, and let us gently reason with the unjust, who is not intentionally in error. 'Sweet Sir,' we will say to him, 'what think you of things esteemed noble and ignoble? Is not the noble that which subjects the beast to the man, or rather to the god in man; and the ignoble that which subjects the man to the beast?' He can hardly avoid saying yes -- can he now?"
refashioned:
"Certainly -- that's what the defender of injustice is effectively saying."

"The defender of justice replies: speak and act so as to give the inner man the fullest possible mastery over the whole creature. Tend the many-headed beast like a good farmer -- cultivate the gentle qualities, prevent the wild ones from growing. Make the lion your ally. Through careful attention to all three, unite them with each other and with yourself."

"Yes, that's exactly what the defender of justice says."

"So from every angle -- pleasure, honor, and practical advantage -- the defender of justice is right, truthful, and knowledgeable, while the defender of injustice is wrong, false, and ignorant."

"From every angle."

"Now let us reason gently with the unjust man, who isn't deliberately in error. 'My good fellow,' we'll say, 'what do you think makes something noble or ignoble? Isn't the noble what subjects the beast to the man -- or rather, to the god within man? And isn't the ignoble what subjects the man to the beast?' He can hardly disagree, can he?"
elegant: Justice means giving the inner man mastery -- tending the beast like a farmer, making the lion an ally, uniting all parts in harmony. From every perspective -- pleasure, honor, advantage -- justice is right and injustice wrong. The noble is what subjects the beast to the god in man; the ignoble reverses this.
summary: The defender of justice advocates giving reason full mastery, tending appetites like a good farmer, and allying with spirit to create inner harmony. From every angle -- pleasure, honor, advantage -- justice is vindicated. Socrates then gently addresses the unjust man: nobility means subjecting the beast to the god within; ignobility is the reverse.
distilled: Justice is reason's mastery over the inner beast, cultivating harmony; from every perspective -- pleasure, honor, advantage -- the just life is superior.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Good husbandman' (georgos) -- the agricultural metaphor for soul-tending connects to the nurture of the guardians in Books II-III -- 'Making the lion-heart his ally' -- spirit serves reason, as the auxiliaries serve the philosopher-rulers in the city -- 'From every point of view' summarizes all three proofs: pleasure (Third Proof), honor (Second Proof), advantage (First Proof) -- 'The god in man' elevates reason to the divine -- Cross-ref: Timaeus 90a on the divine element in the soul -- 'Sweet Sir' (o daimonie) is a gentle address to the mistaken -- Socratic persuasion rather than coercion -- 'Not intentionally in error' reflects the Socratic principle that no one does wrong willingly (Protagoras 345d-e)

narrative:
"Not if he has any regard for my opinion."

"But, if he agree so far, we may ask him to answer another question: 'Then how would a man profit if he received gold and silver on the condition that he was to enslave the noblest part of him to the worst? Who can imagine that a man who sold his son or daughter into slavery for money, especially if he sold them into the hands of fierce and evil men, would be the gainer, however large might be the sum which he received? And will any one say that he is not a miserable caitiff who remorselessly sells his own divine being to that which is most godless and detestable? Eriphyle took the necklace as the price of her husband's life, but he is taking a bribe in order to compass a worse ruin.'"
refashioned:
"Not if he respects my judgment."

"Then we press further: 'How could a man profit by receiving gold and silver if the condition is enslaving the noblest part of himself to the worst? Imagine someone selling his own son or daughter into slavery -- into the hands of brutal, evil men. Would anyone call him the winner, no matter how much money he received? Then can anyone claim it's not miserable to sell one's own divine inner nature to what is most godless and detestable? Eriphyle accepted a golden necklace as the price of her husband's life -- but this man takes a bribe to bring about a far worse destruction.'"
elegant: How could gold and silver profit a man if the price is enslaving his divine nature to what is most base? Eriphyle sold her husband's life for a necklace; the unjust man sells his own soul for a worse ruin.
summary: Socrates argues that no amount of wealth can compensate for enslaving the soul's noblest part to its worst. Just as no one would profit from selling their children into slavery, no one profits from selling their divine inner nature. Even Eriphyle's infamous bargain is less ruinous.
distilled: No amount of gold compensates for enslaving the divine part of the soul to the bestial -- a worse bargain than Eriphyle's betrayal.
notes: Socrates speaking -- Eriphyle was bribed with a golden necklace (the necklace of Harmonia) by Polynices to persuade her husband Amphiaraus to join the expedition against Thebes, knowing he would die -- a famous mythological example of fatal greed -- 'His own divine being' (to theiotaton) -- reason as the divine element in humans -- 'Sold his son or daughter into slavery' makes the abstract argument emotionally vivid -- Cross-ref: Book II's challenge about whether injustice with good reputation profits (360e-362c) -- The argument structure: if selling others into slavery for money is obviously wrong, selling the best part of yourself is worse -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 477a-479e on injustice as the greatest evil of the soul

narrative:
"Yes," said Glaucon, "far worse -- I will answer for him."

"Has not the intemperate been censured of old, because in him the huge multiform monster is allowed to be too much at large?"

"Clearly."

"And men are blamed for pride and bad temper when the lion and serpent element in them disproportionately grows and gains strength?"

"Yes."

"And luxury and softness are blamed, because they relax and weaken this same creature, and make a coward of him?"

"Very true."

"And is not a man reproached for flattery and meanness who subordinates the spirited animal to the unruly monster, and, for the sake of money, of which he can never have enough, habituates him in the days of his youth to be trampled in the mire, and from being a lion to become a monkey?"

"True," he said.

"And why are mean employments and manual arts a reproach? Only because they imply a natural weakness of the higher principle; the individual is unable to control the creatures within him, but has to court them, and his great study is how to flatter them."
refashioned:
"Yes," said Glaucon, "far worse -- I'll answer for him."

"Hasn't intemperance always been condemned because it lets the huge, many-headed monster roam free?"

"Clearly."

"And aren't pride and hot-headedness condemned because the lion and serpent element grows disproportionately strong?"

"Yes."

"And luxury and softness are condemned because they relax and weaken that same spirited creature, turning it into a coward?"

"Very true."

"And isn't a person reproached for flattery and servility when they subordinate the lion-spirit to the unruly monster -- for the sake of money they can never get enough of -- training it from youth to be trampled in the mud, transforming a lion into a monkey?"

"True."

"And why are lowly occupations and manual labor looked down upon? Only because they indicate a weakness of the higher principle. The person cannot control the creatures within and must instead court and flatter them."
elegant: Each vice corresponds to a disorder in the soul-image. Intemperance unleashes the beast; pride over-strengthens the lion; luxury weakens it into cowardice; flattery degrades the lion into a monkey serving the beast. Manual labor is scorned when it signals reason's inability to govern the inner creatures.
summary: Socrates maps traditional vices onto the soul-image: intemperance unleashes the beast; pride and hot-headedness over-strengthen the lion; luxury weakens it; flattery and servility degrade the lion into a monkey serving appetite. Lowly occupations are scorned because they indicate reason's inability to govern.
distilled: Each vice is a specific disorder of the soul-image: the beast too free, the lion too strong or too weak, or reason unable to govern at all.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- Systematic mapping of traditional Greek vices onto the tripartite soul-image: intemperance = beast uncontrolled; pride/bad temper = lion/serpent overgrown; luxury = lion weakened; flattery = lion subordinated to beast -- 'From being a lion to become a monkey' -- the degradation of spirit into sycophancy is a vivid image -- 'Serpent element' (drakontodes) added to the lion suggests the spirited part can be noble (lion) or base (serpent) -- The critique of manual labor reflects Athenian aristocratic prejudice, but Socrates gives it a philosophical rationale: weakness of rational governance -- Cross-ref: Book IV (441e-442b) on spirit as reason's natural ally -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 521d-522a on flattery vs. genuine arts

narrative:
"Such appears to be the reason."

"And therefore, being desirous of placing him under a rule like that of the best, we say that he ought to be the servant of the best, in whom the Divine rules; not, as Thrasymachus supposed, to the injury of the servant, but because every one had better be ruled by divine wisdom dwelling within him; or, if this be impossible, then by an external authority, in order that we may be all, as far as possible, under the same government, friends and equals."
refashioned:
"That seems to be the reason."

"And this is why we say that people who cannot govern themselves should be governed by the best -- those in whom the divine principle rules. Not, as Thrasymachus argued, for the ruler's benefit at the servant's expense, but because everyone is better off ruled by divine wisdom. Ideally, that wisdom dwells within each person. If that's impossible, then it must come from an external authority -- so that we may all, as far as possible, live under the same governance, as friends and equals."
elegant: Everyone is better off governed by divine wisdom -- ideally from within, otherwise from external authority. This is not the exploitation Thrasymachus imagined, but a governance that makes all people friends and equals under the same rational principle.
summary: Socrates argues that those unable to govern themselves should be governed by the best, in whom divine wisdom rules. This is not exploitation, as Thrasymachus claimed, but benefits the governed. Ideally wisdom rules from within; if not, external authority serves the same purpose -- making all friends and equals.
distilled: Governance by divine wisdom -- whether internal or external -- benefits the governed and makes all people friends and equals, contra Thrasymachus.
notes: Socrates speaking -- Direct response to Thrasymachus' argument in Book I (338c-339a) that justice is the advantage of the stronger -- 'Not to the injury of the servant' explicitly refutes Thrasymachus -- 'Divine rules' (to theion archei) -- reason as the divine principle within -- Cross-ref: Book I, Thrasymachus' challenge (338c-354c) -- Cross-ref: Book IV (441e) on reason's rightful rule -- 'Friends and equals' (philoi kai homoioi) describes the ideal political relationship under rational governance -- The internal/external governance distinction maps onto the philosopher-king and ordinary citizens: philosophers are self-governed, others need the city's laws -- Cross-ref: Laws 645a on humans as puppets of the gods -- This passage grounds political authority in the individual's need for rational governance

narrative:
"True," he said.

"And this is clearly seen to be the intention of the law, which is the ally of the whole city; and is seen also in the authority which we exercise over children, and the refusal to let them be free until we have established in them a principle analogous to the constitution of a state, and by cultivation of this higher element have set up in their hearts a guardian and ruler like our own, and when this is done they may go their ways."

"Yes," he said, "the purpose of the law is manifest."

"From what point of view, then, and on what ground can we say that a man is profited by injustice or intemperance or other baseness, which will make him a worse man, even though he acquire money or power by his wickedness?"
refashioned:
"True."

"And this is clearly the purpose of law -- it's the ally of the whole city. You can see it also in how we raise children: we don't grant them freedom until we've established within them a principle analogous to a city's constitution. By cultivating their higher element, we set up a guardian and ruler in their hearts that mirrors our own. Only then do we let them go their way."

"Yes, the law's purpose is clear."

"So from what perspective, on what possible ground, could anyone claim that injustice, intemperance, or any other vice profits a person -- when it makes them worse, even if they gain money or power through their wickedness?"
elegant: Law is the city's ally, and its purpose mirrors child-rearing: cultivate the higher principle within until it can self-govern. From no perspective can injustice or vice profit anyone, since it makes them worse regardless of external gains.
summary: Law serves as the city's ally, just as parenting cultivates the rational principle in children until they can self-govern. Once this inner guardian is established, they are ready for freedom. No perspective can justify injustice, since it degrades the person regardless of any material gain.
distilled: Law and education both aim to establish inner rational governance; no external gain from injustice can compensate for the soul's degradation.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Ally of the whole city' -- law (nomos) as the external counterpart to internal rational governance -- Education as establishing an internal 'constitution' -- the micro/macro parallel of soul and city -- 'Guardian and ruler like our own' -- the rational principle in the child mirrors the philosopher-rulers in the city -- 'When this is done they may go their ways' -- freedom requires prior rational formation -- Cross-ref: Book II-III on the education of the guardians -- Cross-ref: Book VII on the philosopher's education and return to govern -- Cross-ref: Laws 653a-b on education as establishing correct pleasures and pains in youth -- The rhetorical question ('From what point of view...') effectively closes the argument: there is no ground for defending injustice

narrative:
"From no point of view at all."

"What shall he profit, if his injustice be undetected and unpunished? He who is undetected only gets worse, whereas he who is detected and punished has the brutal part of his nature silenced and humanized; the gentler element in him is liberated, and his whole soul is perfected and ennobled by the acquirement of justice and temperance and wisdom, more than the body ever is by receiving gifts of beauty, strength and health, in proportion as the soul is more honourable than the body."

"Certainly," he said.

"To this nobler purpose the man of understanding will devote the energies of his life. And in the first place, he will honour studies which impress these qualities on his soul and disregard others?"
refashioned:
"From no perspective whatsoever."

"What does the unjust man gain if his injustice goes undetected and unpunished? The undetected criminal only gets worse. But the one who is caught and punished has the brutal part of his nature silenced and humanized. The gentler element is liberated. His whole soul is perfected and ennobled through acquiring justice, temperance, and wisdom -- a greater transformation than the body ever achieves through beauty, strength, or health, in proportion as the soul is more worthy than the body."

"Certainly."

"The person of understanding will devote their life's energy to this nobler purpose. First, they will honor studies that cultivate these qualities in the soul and disregard the rest."
elegant: Undetected injustice only worsens the criminal. Punishment silences the brutal nature and liberates the gentle. Justice, temperance, and wisdom perfect the soul more than beauty and strength perfect the body. The wise person devotes their life to soul-cultivation above all.
summary: Undetected injustice makes the unjust person worse, not better. Detection and punishment actually benefit the criminal by silencing the brutal element and liberating the gentle. Justice, temperance, and wisdom perfect the soul more than beauty perfects the body. The wise person devotes their life to soul-cultivating studies.
distilled: Undetected injustice only worsens the soul; punishment benefits the unjust by humanizing the brutal element and perfecting the whole soul.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Undetected only gets worse' directly answers Book II's challenge (360b-362c) about the unjust man with a just reputation -- even if never caught, he is harmed -- Punishment as therapeutic, not merely retributive -- anticipates rehabilitative justice theory -- 'The brutal part silenced and humanized' -- punishment tames the beast in the soul-image -- 'Soul more honourable than the body' -- the consistent Platonic hierarchy -- Cross-ref: Gorgias 477a-479e on punishment as the medicine of the soul -- Cross-ref: Crito 47d-48a on the importance of the soul's condition over the body's -- This completes the answer to Thrasymachus and Glaucon/Adeimantus: justice benefits the soul intrinsically

narrative:
"Clearly," he said.

"In the next place, he will regulate his bodily habit and training, and so far will he be from yielding to brutal and irrational pleasures, that he will regard even health as quite a secondary matter; his first object will be not that he may be fair or strong or well, unless he is likely thereby to gain temperance, but he will always desire so to attemper the body as to preserve the harmony of the soul?"
refashioned:
"Clearly."

"Next, the wise person will regulate their physical habits and training. Far from surrendering to brutal, irrational pleasures, they will consider even health a secondary matter. Their goal won't be beauty, strength, or physical fitness for their own sake -- only insofar as these contribute to temperance. They will always seek to attune the body in a way that preserves the soul's harmony."
elegant: The wise person treats even health as secondary, valuing physical fitness only when it serves the soul's harmony. The body is attuned not for its own sake but to preserve inner temperance.
summary: The wise person regulates physical training not for beauty or strength but to maintain the soul's harmony. Even health is secondary to temperance; the body is always attuned in service of the soul.
distilled: The wise person values physical health only insofar as it preserves the soul's harmony and temperance.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'Attemper the body to preserve the harmony of the soul' encapsulates the soul-body hierarchy -- 'Even health as secondary' is a strong claim: bodily goods are instrumental, not intrinsic -- 'Brutal and irrational pleasures' -- the beast in the soul-image -- Cross-ref: Book III (403c-412b) on the physical education of the guardians -- gymnastic serves the soul, not the body alone -- Cross-ref: Phaedo 64d-65a on the philosopher's relative indifference to bodily pleasures -- The passage describes the practical ethics of the philosophical life: how the just person actually lives

narrative:
"Certainly he will, if he has true music in him."

"And in the acquisition of wealth there is a principle of order and harmony which he will also observe; he will not allow himself to be dazzled by the foolish applause of the world, and heap up riches to his own infinite harm?"

"Certainly not," he said.

"He will look at the city which is within him, and take heed that no disorder occur in it, such as might arise either from superfluity or from want; and upon this principle he will regulate his property and gain or spend according to his means."

"Very true."

"And, for the same reason, he will gladly accept and enjoy such honours as he deems likely to make him a better man; but those, whether private or public, which are likely to disorder his life, he will avoid?"

"Then, if that is his motive, he will not be a statesman."

"By the dog of Egypt, he will! in the city which is his own he certainly will, though in the land of his birth perhaps not, unless he have a divine call."

"I understand; you mean that he will be a ruler in the city of which we are the founders, and which exists in idea only; for I do not believe that there is such an one anywhere on earth?"

"In heaven," Socrates replied, "there is laid up a pattern of it, methinks, which he who desires may behold, and beholding, may set his own house in order. But whether such an one exists, or ever will exist in fact, is no matter; for he will live after the manner of that city, having nothing to do with any other."

"I think so," he said.
refashioned:
"He certainly will, if he has genuine culture in him."

"And in acquiring wealth, he'll observe a principle of order and harmony. He won't let himself be dazzled by the world's foolish applause and pile up riches to his own harm."

"Certainly not."

"He'll attend to the city within himself, ensuring no disorder arises from either excess or deficiency. On this principle he'll manage his property -- earning and spending according to his means."

"Very true."

"And for the same reason, he'll welcome honors that make him a better person, but avoid any -- public or private -- that would disorder his inner life."

"Then by that logic, he won't enter politics."

"By the dog of Egypt, he absolutely will! He will be a statesman in his own inner city. In the land of his birth, perhaps not -- unless he receives a divine calling."

"I see. You mean he'll be a ruler in the ideal city we've been constructing -- which exists only in theory. I don't believe such a city exists anywhere on earth."

"In heaven," Socrates replied, "there is laid up a pattern of it. Anyone who wishes may look upon it and, beholding it, set their own inner house in order. Whether this city exists or ever will exist on earth doesn't matter. The just person will live by the principles of that city alone, and no other."

"I think so."
elegant: The just person manages wealth, honors, and politics by one principle: preserving the inner city's order. He will be a statesman in his own soul, even if not in earthly politics. In heaven there is a pattern of the ideal city; whoever beholds it may set their own house in order. Whether it exists on earth is irrelevant -- the just person lives by its principles alone.
summary: The just person regulates wealth and honors to maintain inner order, governing the 'city within.' Socrates insists the philosopher will govern -- in the inner city, if not the earthly one. The ideal city exists as a heavenly pattern; whether it is ever realized on earth is irrelevant, for the just person lives by its principles regardless.
distilled: The ideal city exists as a heavenly pattern; the just person lives by its principles in the city of their own soul, whether or not it ever exists on earth.
notes: Socrates and Glaucon speaking -- 'True music' (mousike) means genuine cultural education, not just literal music -- 'City which is within him' -- the internalized ideal city, the ultimate application of the city-soul analogy -- 'By the dog of Egypt' -- Socrates' characteristic oath, perhaps referencing Anubis -- 'In heaven there is laid up a pattern' (paradeigma en ourano) -- the ideal city as a transcendent Form, the Republic's political metaphysics -- 'Set his own house in order' -- the practical takeaway: the ideal city's value is as an inner guide, not necessarily an external institution -- Cross-ref: Book V-VII on the philosopher-king and the ideal city's possibility -- Cross-ref: Book VII (540d-541b) on whether the ideal city can be realized -- 'Having nothing to do with any other' -- the just person's independence from corrupt political orders -- This is the Republic's concluding vision for Book IX: justice as inner governance modeled on the heavenly pattern
